content.content:
280	standby is exhausted the controller cycles back to index 0 and keeps retrying every
281	endpoint in order. Wrap is chosen over *stop* to preserve availability — a hub that
282	recovers earlier in the list is retried and settled on within one cycle, whereas
283	stopping at the last endpoint would strand the edge on a dead hub even after
284	endpoint 0 came back. The settle dwell keeps the round-robin a slow, bounded retry,
285	not a storm.
286	
287	**GUARD (must-hold invariant).** A **single-endpoint** list takes **no** failover
288	action — no advance, no remote repoint, no re-handshake. A one-concentrator
289	deployment (including the legacy single `endpoint` form, normalized to a
290	one-element list) is therefore byte-for-byte the pre-T57 behaviour. The switch and
291	this guard are validated by the real-network netns e2e (`TestHubFailoverStandbySwitch`
292	+ `TestHubFailoverSingleEndpointGuard`, T62) and, over the real internet, by the
293	realhosts mid-transfer WAN-kill tier (`TestRealMidTransferWANKill`, T63).
294	
295	**Re-resolution** (`resolution`, T73). A hostname endpoint spec has no fixed
296	address; its expansion is a **mutable, spec-keyed record set** the failover set
297	carries (`failoverSpec.addrs`, updated in place by `hubFailover.updateResolution`
298	under the endpoint-set lock — the sole mutation point). The re-resolution
299	controller keeps those record sets fresh. It mirrors the `hubFailover` shape (a pure
300	constructor over an injected `dnsresolve.Resolver`, the failover controller it
301	drives, a `telemetry.Clock`, and the `[dns]` poll interval + per-lookup timeout) and
302	runs its own device-lifecycle loop **off the send hot path** — all lookups happen on
303	its goroutine; results are applied only through `updateResolution`. Each evaluation:
304	
305	- **Poll** every hostname spec on the fixed `[dns]` cadence; on a **successful,
306	  non-empty** lookup the addrs are **family-filtered then ordered** — addrs of a
307	  family no local path can source (a path binds a socket whose family is fixed by its
308	  `source_addr`, so an AAAA answer on a v4-only edge is unreachable and is dropped),
309	  then IPv4 first, then IPv6, deduped — a deterministic order so an unchanged answer
310	  yields a byte-identical expansion. The result is handed to `updateResolution`, which
311	  **repoints only on an actual active-IP change** (D32 no-op suppression). When the
312	  transport exposes a TTL (DoH/DoT), the next poll is clamped to
313	  `min(pollInterval, minTTL)`.
314	- **Liveness-loss trigger**: the instant every path to the **active** endpoint reads
315	  `StateDown` — the *same* `allDown` sweep the failover loop advances on (Q34: the
316	  two controllers coordinate purely through the shared lock and the update API) — the
317	  active spec is re-resolved **out of band**, edge-triggered, without waiting for the
318	  next poll tick. This out-of-band re-arm is clamped to `min(pollInterval, minTTL)`
319	  too, so record freshness holds on exactly the hub-loss path where it matters most.
320	- **Retention invariant (D46)**: a lookup that **fails** (error/timeout/NXDOMAIN) or
321	  yields an **empty** usable set — including an answer that **filters down to empty**
322	  because it carries no family any local path can source — **never publishes**: the
323	  spec keeps its last-good expansion and the controller retries next tick. A transient
324	  resolver fault, or an answer for a family the edge cannot reach, therefore never
325	  tears down a working endpoint set, and `hubFailover` never sees a previously-resolved
326	  active spec collapse to empty (the condition its `total < 2` guard could otherwise
327	  strand the bond on).
328	
329	This controller runs **even for a single-hostname peer** (to track a changing DDNS
330	address), independent of hub-failover's `>= 2` guard; the first successful resolution
331	of a hostname-only peer is what boot-adopts its active endpoint.
332	
333	**Device lifecycle** (T74). At `Up` the device does one **bounded initial resolve**
334	of each hostname spec (the `[dns]` per-lookup timeout) and builds the engine/UAPI peer
335	endpoint **only from resolved entries** — the flattened head of the seeded specs. If a
336	name does not resolve in the boot window (single-hostname peer, resolver down), the
337	tunnel comes up **without a peer endpoint** (tolerant boot, Q30 defer-and-reconcile —
338	an unresolvable name never hard-fails bring-up); the concentrator already runs
339	endpoint-less, so the engine supports it. The resolver is constructed **once**, and
340	**only when some peer carries a hostname spec** — a zero-hostname config builds no
341	resolver and starts no loop (Q29 inertness). The **first-resolve install path (R70)**
342	is load-bearing: `SetPeerRemote` repoints the bind's per-path remotes but **never sets
343	the engine peer's endpoint**, which is populated **only** by a UAPI `endpoint=` line
344	routed through `Multipath.ParseEndpoint`. So after an endpoint-less boot, the first
345	successful resolve must **install** the resolved endpoint on the engine peer via the
346	UAPI/IpcSet path (`deviceInstallEndpoint`) **then** re-handshake — the initiation now
347	has an addressable endpoint. Subsequent re-resolves of an already-installed peer take
348	the normal `SetPeerRemote` repoint path (the engine's virtual endpoint stays stable per
349	A1; only the bind remotes move). The re-resolution loop's stopper is held on the
350	`Tunnel` and invoked by `Close` between the hub-failover stop and the engine teardown.
351	The whole flow — endpoint-less boot while the name is unresolvable, the R70
352	first-resolve install, a mid-session concentrator-IP change, and the re-resolve repoint
353	whose `SetPeerRemote` re-baselines the receive resequencer so post-change traffic
354	actually resumes (the D32 guard) — is validated end to end by the privileged netns e2e
355	`TestDNSHubResolveAndReroute` (Q36), with a hermetic in-namespace UDP DNS responder as
356	the sole answer source (no external DNS egress).
357	
358	### Per-path telemetry — `internal/telemetry`
359	
360	Measures per-path quality (RTT, loss, jitter) by exchanging authenticated PROBE
361	frames, and drives liveness/failover decisions. Carries the anti-replay
362	primitives (`AntiReplay` high-water, `ControlGuard`) that protect PROBE and
363	CONTROL against replay of captured valid-MAC frames.
364	
365	### Receive resequencer — `internal/reseq`
366	
367	Bonding across paths of different latency reorders packets. The resequencer holds
368	a **bounded window** with a timeout and restores order **before** the inner
369	WireGuard anti-replay window sees the traffic — critical, because WG would
370	otherwise drop legitimately-reordered datagrams. It runs its **own outer sequence
371	space** and never touches the inner WireGuard counter (a core invariant).
372	
373	### FEC — `internal/fec` + `internal/adaptivefec`
374	
375	- `fec` implements Reed-Solomon over groups of *K* data shards + *M* parity
376	  shards (via `klauspost/reedsolomon`). Lost DATA frames are reconstructed from
377	  PARITY without retransmission.
378	- `adaptivefec` is a pure, deterministic control loop that floats *M* in
379	  `[0, ceiling]` to track measured loss. It can be driven by a **`target_residual`
380	  SLA** (invert the binomial residual `E[max(0,D−M)]/K` to the smallest *M*
381	  meeting the target) or a legacy `safety_factor` multiplier (mutually
382	  exclusive). Both off by default; enable with `[fec] enabled = true`
383	  (+ `adaptive = true`).
384	
385	> **Dependency invariant (pinned in `go.mod`):** the adaptive path codes each
386	> group `RS(m, k≤ceiling)` yet decodes against a single `RS(m, ceiling)` codec.
387	> That is byte-exact only because reedsolomon's default matrix makes parity shard
388	> *j* identical across total-parity counts — an *undocumented* property. Any
389	> reedsolomon bump must be re-verified against
390	> `TestKlauspostParityPrefixStableInvariant` (`internal/fec`) before landing.
391	
392	### Supporting packages
393	
394	- `internal/config` — loads the single TOML config, validates fail-fast at load
395	  (0600 perms, complete-or-absent amnezia block, FEC bounds, scheduler
396	  invariants, unique `source_addr`). The optional `[dns]` block selects the
397	  resolver transport (system default, DoH, or DoT) a peer's opt-in hostname
398	  endpoint is resolved through, enforcing the BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant (a
399	  hostname-form `doh_url`/`dot_server` requires an explicit `bootstrap_ip`;
400	  an IP-literal host rejects a non-empty `bootstrap_ip` as a mode mismatch)
401	  and constructing the matching `internal/dnsresolve` implementation — when
402	  `bootstrap_ip` is set, that implementation dials it directly instead of
403	  resolving the configured hostname through the system dialer.
404	- `internal/device` — brings a tunnel up from a validated config (Up/Down/Reload),
405	  wires metrics, handles SIGHUP path add/remove without teardown.
406	- `internal/metrics` — a private-registry Prometheus `/metrics` endpoint that
407	  **refuses any non-loopback bind**.
408	- `internal/wireaudit` — the requirement-6 DPI wire-format audit (pcap parse +
409	  per-offset value-entropy + coverage checks) used by the P5 tests.
410	- `internal/log` — slog-based structured logging.
411	- `internal/dnsresolve` — the DNS resolution seam: a context-bounded `Resolver`
412	  interface, a system implementation over `net.Resolver`, two private-resolver
413	  transports sharing one dnsmessage encode/decode/error taxonomy, and an
414	  in-memory `FakeResolver` for tests. `DoHResolver` is DNS-over-HTTPS (RFC
415	  8484): wire-encoded with `golang.org/x/net/dns/dnsmessage`, POSTed over a
416	  dedicated `http.Client` with standard system CA trust (no
417	  insecure-skip-verify knob). `DoTResolver` is DNS-over-TLS (RFC 7858): one
418	  `crypto/tls` connection per lookup per family, queries framed with the
419	  2-byte length prefix, same system CA trust and server-name verification.
420	  Both query A and AAAA and tolerate one family answering NXDOMAIN when the
421	  other resolves, and both treat an empty final addr set as a typed error
422	  (`NXDomainError`/`NoDataError`), never a silent `([], nil)`. Residual leak
423	  for both: TLS SNI/timing to the configured provider.
424	
425	## DNS endpoints and resolver privacy trade-offs
426	
427	The optional `[dns]` block selects which transport resolves a peer's hostname
428	endpoint when opted in with `dns = true` per-peer. It is **opt-in by default —
429	[dns] alone never enables hostname resolution**; every peer endpoint is always
430	an IP literal unless explicitly marked `dns = true`. Hostname endpoints are
431	resolved through the OS system resolver by default (when `[dns]` is absent).
432	
433	### Why default-off: the DPI thesis
434	
435	A **pre-tunnel hostname lookup** is an unencrypted (cleartext) signal: an
436	on-path adversary sees the edge asking for the public concentrator's hostname
437	*before* the tunnel is up, making the host blocklistable at the DNS level
438	without inspecting any encrypted traffic. This is true regardless of how the
439	lookup is done — system resolver, DoH/DoT — because the resolver *itself*
440	learns the query. The default posture (IP literals only; hostnames deferred
441	to an explicit opt-in with `dns = true` + an explicit `[dns]` resolver block)
442	keeps this leakage off by default and surfaces it as an intentional choice.
443	
444	### Leaked artifacts per resolver mode
445	
446	Once a peer opts into hostname resolution (`dns = true`), the transport choice
447	in `[dns].resolver` determines what information escapes to a passive on-path
448	observer — per Requirement-6 testing (test/e2e/p5_dpi_test.go, Q29/Q33):
449	
450	- **system** (default, OS stub resolver): A **cleartext DNS query naming the
451	  concentrator's hostname** — the full QNAME in plaintext on port 53. This is
452	  the most visible artifact: a DPI engine observes the exact hostname being
453	  resolved and can block it pre-emptively at the network edge.
454	
455	- **DoH** (DNS-over-HTTPS, RFC 8484): The **TLS ClientHello SNI** (Server Name
456	  Indication, naming the DoH provider's host) **plus timing/connection metadata**
457	  to the DoH provider. The query payload itself is encrypted within the HTTPS
458	  tunnel, but the SNI and the observed request/response cadence allow timing-based
459	  inference and correlation to the DoH provider — the concentrator hostname is
460	  *not* visible on the wire.
461	
462	- **DoT** (DNS-over-TLS, RFC 7858): Identical to DoH: the **TLS ClientHello SNI**
463	  (naming the DoT server) **plus timing and connection metadata** to the DoT
464	  provider. The query is encrypted, but SNI + timing correlates the edge to that
465	  resolver.
466	
467	In all three cases, **multi-record expansions** (a hostname resolving to multiple
468	A/AAAA records, e.g. concentrator failover `endpoints` list) feed back into
469	hub-failover: each address in the result set feeds the active-backup or weighted
470	scheduler as a separate routable endpoint, so the edge can failover within that
471	set on liveness loss (one address down → try the next).
472	
473	### Opt-in defer-and-reconcile boot semantics
474	
475	A hostname endpoint that cannot be resolved at startup (resolver down, DNS
476	outage, network unreachable) **never blocks tunnel bring-up**. The tunnel
477	boots without that endpoint, and a background re-resolution loop (at the
478	cadence `[dns].poll_interval`, default 30s) installs it and initiates the
479	Noise handshake on the first successful lookup. Steady-state re-resolution then
480	repoints the bond whenever the hostname's record changes (TTL-driven or faster,
481	per the operational `poll_interval`).
482	
483	- **Re-resolution cadence**: `[dns].poll_interval` (default 30s, must be > 0).
484	  Governs how often an opted-in hostname endpoint is re-resolved; changes are
485	  reconciled immediately.
486	
487	- **Liveness-loss trigger**: If all endpoints (resolved A/AAAA records + any
488	  IP-literal endpoints) for a peer go unreachable simultaneously, the
489	  re-resolution loop shortens its cadence and retries aggressively; once any
490	  endpoint recovers, it returns to the normal cadence.
491	
492	- **Change suppression**: Re-resolution results that are identical to the prior
493	  set (same IP + port, same order) do not trigger re-keying or re-handshake —
494	  they are silent, minimizing control churn.
495	
496	### Mixing rules with ordered endpoints
497	
498	When a peer declares multiple endpoints via the `endpoints` list (hub-failover),
499	and one or more are hostnames (`dns = true`), the following rules apply:
500	
501	1. **Each hostname expands to its full A+AAAA record set** at resolve time; IP
502	   literals and resolved addresses are all ordered together per the scheduler's
503	   active-backup or weighted policy.
504	
505	2. **Order preservation**: the `endpoints` list order is **strict** — the scheduler
506	   respects it for active-backup failover (index 0 is primary, index N are standbys).
507	   When a hostname at index K resolves to multiple addresses, they are inserted
508	   *consecutively* in that order (first address fills index K, subsequent addresses
509	   shift later entries right in the ordering).
510	
511	3. **Deduplication**: if two `endpoints` entries (whether IP or hostname) resolve
512	   to the same address:port, both are rejected at config load (duplicates not
513	   allowed in the flattened set).
514	
515	4. **Resolver selection is global**: `[dns].resolver` applies to *all* opted-in
516	   hostname endpoints — you cannot mix system + DoH + DoT within one config.
517	   Choose one transport per tunnel.
518	
519	## Load-bearing invariants
520	
521	These are the rules that keep the design correct; break them and the tunnel
522	misbehaves subtly. Agents and contributors must preserve them.
523	
524	1. **One virtual endpoint per peer (A1).** The engine sees a single stable
525	   `Endpoint`; the Bind fans out beneath it. Never surface per-path endpoint
526	   churn to the engine.
527	2. **Own outer sequence space.** The resequencer/FEC use wanbond's outer-seq;
528	   never reuse or perturb the inner WireGuard counter.
529	3. **Resequence before inner anti-replay.** Restore cross-path order in `reseq`
530	   before the engine's replay window validates.
531	4. **Inner fail-closed; outer control authenticated.** WireGuard authenticates the
532	   payload; PROBE/CONTROL are PSK-HMAC authenticated with monotonic anti-replay;
533	   DATA/PARITY are deliberately unauthenticated (see Security model).
534	5. **Amnezia is isolated to `bind.go`.** All engine coupling goes through the type
535	   aliases there.
536	6. **Amnezia is all-or-nothing** and **single-engine-per-process** (its magic-
537	   header logic uses package-level globals). Config validation enforces the
538	   former; deployments must honor the latter.
539	7. **Re-verify the reedsolomon prefix invariant** on any FEC-dependency bump.
540	
541	## Security model
542	
543	- **Payload**: confidentiality, integrity, authenticity provided by inner
544	  WireGuard (Noise + AEAD). wanbond never sees plaintext.
545	- **Outer control plane** (PROBE, CONTROL): PSK-HMAC authenticated + per-peer
546	  monotonic anti-replay — an attacker cannot forge or replay them.
547	- **Outer data plane** (DATA, PARITY): **unauthenticated by design**. A network
548	  attacker can forge/replay these; forgeries are dropped by the inner AEAD (real
549	  payload) or discarded by the FEC decoder as inconsistent. The accepted residual
550	  risk is **DoS-grade only** (an attacker can waste decode/resequence work), never
551	  a confidentiality or integrity break. This trade buys ~0 per-packet overhead on
552	  the hot path.
553	- **Traffic analysis / DPI**: the outer wire has no fingerprint (random nonce,
554	  obfuscated body, no magic bytes); AmneziaWG junk params add defense-in-depth.
555	  Protocol *mimicry* (looking like HTTPS) is an explicit non-goal.
556	
557	## Not yet built / deliberate boundaries
558	
559	These are recorded design boundaries, not defects:
560	
561	- **No live CONTROL protocol.** The CONTROL frame kind and its `ControlGuard`
562	  anti-replay exist and are tested, but inbound CONTROL is currently dropped at
563	  the Bind (`multipath.go` receive default case). It is the chokepoint a future
564	  out-of-band signalling layer (e.g. explicit rekey/state) must route through.
565	- **Pacing ships disabled by default; no live auto-tuning (Q20).** Empirical
566	  sizing *is* built: `SizePacingFromBDP` derives the per-path pace from an
567	  operator-declared per-link bandwidth (`link_bandwidth`/`link_rtt`) at config
568	  load (T53), and enabled-pacing was measured to eliminate bufferbloat both on the
569	  bandwidth-capped netns fixture (T56/T61) and on the real-link tier (T58). What
570	  stays a **deliberate boundary**: pacing is off unless `[scheduler] pacing_enabled
571	  = true`, and the declared bandwidth is fixed at load — wanbond never re-derives
572	  the pace live from runtime measurements (Q20 rejected a live control loop for the
573	  pilot). Absent a declared bandwidth (or with pacing off) the default per-path
574	  capacity is synthetic (~115 Mbit/s), well above realistic uplinks.
575	- **In-fixture throughput/bufferbloat measurement is CPU-bound (a fixture
576	  boundary).** The netns fixture proves *functional* bonding/FEC/failover/DPI but
577	  is CPU/PPS-bound, so absolute "bonded ≈ sum of links" throughput and bufferbloat
578	  are **not** measured there — they are measured on the **real-link tier**. The
579	  capped-fixture BDP sub-test (T52) and the realhosts tier (`just p0-baseline` →
580	  `TestRealAggregationBufferbloat` / `TestRealMidTransferWANKill`, T58/T63) record
581	  the aggregation ratio and loaded-vs-idle RTT **report-only**. Note the realhosts
582	  topology shares a single physical uplink, so the measured aggregation ratio is
583	  ~≤1 — this is an informational, report-only measurement, not a bandwidth-
584	  aggregation guarantee (see
585	  [manual-checklist.md §P0](manual-checklist.md#p0--automated-real-link-baseline-realhosts-tier)
586	  and [p0-findings.md](p0-findings.md)).
587	- **Multi-concentrator hub-failover: UDP-only remains a non-goal.** Q18 brought
588	  edge-side ORDERED-ENDPOINT ACTIVE-STANDBY hub failover into scope; the config
589	  surface (T54), the switch (T57), the netns e2e (T62), and the real-link
590	  mid-transfer WAN-kill tier (T63) are all built and validated (see *Concentrator
591	  hub failover* above). What stays out of scope: UDP-only is deliberate — there is
592	  no TCP/TLS fallback for wholesale-UDP-block networks — and the endpoint list is
593	  IP:port only (no DNS resolution).
594	
595	## References
596	
597	- [p0-findings.md](p0-findings.md) — the P0 spike that fixed the single-endpoint,
598	  resequencing, and fixture-is-CPU-bound decisions.
599	- [p0-checkpoint.md](p0-checkpoint.md) — phase gate + deferred-work record.
600	- [install.md](install.md) — deployment and operation.
601	- [manual-checklist.md](manual-checklist.md) — manual per-phase + real-link
602	  verification.
603

file.content:
standby is exhausted the controller cycles back to index 0 and keeps retrying every
endpoint in order. Wrap is chosen over *stop* to preserve availability — a hub that
recovers earlier in the list is retried and settled on within one cycle, whereas
stopping at the last endpoint would strand the edge on a dead hub even after
endpoint 0 came back. The settle dwell keeps the round-robin a slow, bounded retry,
not a storm.

**GUARD (must-hold invariant).** A **single-endpoint** list takes **no** failover
action — no advance, no remote repoint, no re-handshake. A one-concentrator
deployment (including the legacy single `endpoint` form, normalized to a
one-element list) is therefore byte-for-byte the pre-T57 behaviour. The switch and
this guard are validated by the real-network netns e2e (`TestHubFailoverStandbySwitch`
+ `TestHubFailoverSingleEndpointGuard`, T62) and, over the real internet, by the
realhosts mid-transfer WAN-kill tier (`TestRealMidTransferWANKill`, T63).

**Re-resolution** (`resolution`, T73). A hostname endpoint spec has no fixed
address; its expansion is a **mutable, spec-keyed record set** the failover set
carries (`failoverSpec.addrs`, updated in place by `hubFailover.updateResolution`
under the endpoint-set lock — the sole mutation point). The re-resolution
controller keeps those record sets fresh. It mirrors the `hubFailover` shape (a pure
constructor over an injected `dnsresolve.Resolver`, the failover controller it
drives, a `telemetry.Clock`, and the `[dns]` poll interval + per-lookup timeout) and
runs its own device-lifecycle loop **off the send hot path** — all lookups happen on
its goroutine; results are applied only through `updateResolution`. Each evaluation:

- **Poll** every hostname spec on the fixed `[dns]` cadence; on a **successful,
  non-empty** lookup the addrs are **family-filtered then ordered** — addrs of a
  family no local path can source (a path binds a socket whose family is fixed by its
  `source_addr`, so an AAAA answer on a v4-only edge is unreachable and is dropped),
  then IPv4 first, then IPv6, deduped — a deterministic order so an unchanged answer
  yields a byte-identical expansion. The result is handed to `updateResolution`, which
  **repoints only on an actual active-IP change** (D32 no-op suppression). When the
  transport exposes a TTL (DoH/DoT), the next poll is clamped to
  `min(pollInterval, minTTL)`.
- **Liveness-loss trigger**: the instant every path to the **active** endpoint reads
  `StateDown` — the *same* `allDown` sweep the failover loop advances on (Q34: the
  two controllers coordinate purely through the shared lock and the update API) — the
  active spec is re-resolved **out of band**, edge-triggered, without waiting for the
  next poll tick. This out-of-band re-arm is clamped to `min(pollInterval, minTTL)`
  too, so record freshness holds on exactly the hub-loss path where it matters most.
- **Retention invariant (D46)**: a lookup that **fails** (error/timeout/NXDOMAIN) or
  yields an **empty** usable set — including an answer that **filters down to empty**
  because it carries no family any local path can source — **never publishes**: the
  spec keeps its last-good expansion and the controller retries next tick. A transient
  resolver fault, or an answer for a family the edge cannot reach, therefore never
  tears down a working endpoint set, and `hubFailover` never sees a previously-resolved
  active spec collapse to empty (the condition its `total < 2` guard could otherwise
  strand the bond on).

This controller runs **even for a single-hostname peer** (to track a changing DDNS
address), independent of hub-failover's `>= 2` guard; the first successful resolution
of a hostname-only peer is what boot-adopts its active endpoint.

**Device lifecycle** (T74). At `Up` the device does one **bounded initial resolve**
of each hostname spec (the `[dns]` per-lookup timeout) and builds the engine/UAPI peer
endpoint **only from resolved entries** — the flattened head of the seeded specs. If a
name does not resolve in the boot window (single-hostname peer, resolver down), the
tunnel comes up **without a peer endpoint** (tolerant boot, Q30 defer-and-reconcile —
an unresolvable name never hard-fails bring-up); the concentrator already runs
endpoint-less, so the engine supports it. The resolver is constructed **once**, and
**only when some peer carries a hostname spec** — a zero-hostname config builds no
resolver and starts no loop (Q29 inertness). The **first-resolve install path (R70)**
is load-bearing: `SetPeerRemote` repoints the bind's per-path remotes but **never sets
the engine peer's endpoint**, which is populated **only** by a UAPI `endpoint=` line
routed through `Multipath.ParseEndpoint`. So after an endpoint-less boot, the first
successful resolve must **install** the resolved endpoint on the engine peer via the
UAPI/IpcSet path (`deviceInstallEndpoint`) **then** re-handshake — the initiation now
has an addressable endpoint. Subsequent re-resolves of an already-installed peer take
the normal `SetPeerRemote` repoint path (the engine's virtual endpoint stays stable per
A1; only the bind remotes move). The re-resolution loop's stopper is held on the
`Tunnel` and invoked by `Close` between the hub-failover stop and the engine teardown.
The whole flow — endpoint-less boot while the name is unresolvable, the R70
first-resolve install, a mid-session concentrator-IP change, and the re-resolve repoint
whose `SetPeerRemote` re-baselines the receive resequencer so post-change traffic
actually resumes (the D32 guard) — is validated end to end by the privileged netns e2e
`TestDNSHubResolveAndReroute` (Q36), with a hermetic in-namespace UDP DNS responder as
the sole answer source (no external DNS egress).

### Per-path telemetry — `internal/telemetry`

Measures per-path quality (RTT, loss, jitter) by exchanging authenticated PROBE
frames, and drives liveness/failover decisions. Carries the anti-replay
primitives (`AntiReplay` high-water, `ControlGuard`) that protect PROBE and
CONTROL against replay of captured valid-MAC frames.

### Receive resequencer — `internal/reseq`

Bonding across paths of different latency reorders packets. The resequencer holds
a **bounded window** with a timeout and restores order **before** the inner
WireGuard anti-replay window sees the traffic — critical, because WG would
otherwise drop legitimately-reordered datagrams. It runs its **own outer sequence
space** and never touches the inner WireGuard counter (a core invariant).

### FEC — `internal/fec` + `internal/adaptivefec`

- `fec` implements Reed-Solomon over groups of *K* data shards + *M* parity
  shards (via `klauspost/reedsolomon`). Lost DATA frames are reconstructed from
  PARITY without retransmission.
- `adaptivefec` is a pure, deterministic control loop that floats *M* in
  `[0, ceiling]` to track measured loss. It can be driven by a **`target_residual`
  SLA** (invert the binomial residual `E[max(0,D−M)]/K` to the smallest *M*
  meeting the target) or a legacy `safety_factor` multiplier (mutually
  exclusive). Both off by default; enable with `[fec] enabled = true`
  (+ `adaptive = true`).

> **Dependency invariant (pinned in `go.mod`):** the adaptive path codes each
> group `RS(m, k≤ceiling)` yet decodes against a single `RS(m, ceiling)` codec.
> That is byte-exact only because reedsolomon's default matrix makes parity shard
> *j* identical across total-parity counts — an *undocumented* property. Any
> reedsolomon bump must be re-verified against
> `TestKlauspostParityPrefixStableInvariant` (`internal/fec`) before landing.

### Supporting packages

- `internal/config` — loads the single TOML config, validates fail-fast at load
  (0600 perms, complete-or-absent amnezia block, FEC bounds, scheduler
  invariants, unique `source_addr`). The optional `[dns]` block selects the
  resolver transport (system default, DoH, or DoT) a peer's opt-in hostname
  endpoint is resolved through, enforcing the BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant (a
  hostname-form `doh_url`/`dot_server` requires an explicit `bootstrap_ip`;
  an IP-literal host rejects a non-empty `bootstrap_ip` as a mode mismatch)
  and constructing the matching `internal/dnsresolve` implementation — when
  `bootstrap_ip` is set, that implementation dials it directly instead of
  resolving the configured hostname through the system dialer.
- `internal/device` — brings a tunnel up from a validated config (Up/Down/Reload),
  wires metrics, handles SIGHUP path add/remove without teardown.
- `internal/metrics` — a private-registry Prometheus `/metrics` endpoint that
  **refuses any non-loopback bind**.
- `internal/wireaudit` — the requirement-6 DPI wire-format audit (pcap parse +
  per-offset value-entropy + coverage checks) used by the P5 tests.
- `internal/log` — slog-based structured logging.
- `internal/dnsresolve` — the DNS resolution seam: a context-bounded `Resolver`
  interface, a system implementation over `net.Resolver`, two private-resolver
  transports sharing one dnsmessage encode/decode/error taxonomy, and an
  in-memory `FakeResolver` for tests. `DoHResolver` is DNS-over-HTTPS (RFC
  8484): wire-encoded with `golang.org/x/net/dns/dnsmessage`, POSTed over a
  dedicated `http.Client` with standard system CA trust (no
  insecure-skip-verify knob). `DoTResolver` is DNS-over-TLS (RFC 7858): one
  `crypto/tls` connection per lookup per family, queries framed with the
  2-byte length prefix, same system CA trust and server-name verification.
  Both query A and AAAA and tolerate one family answering NXDOMAIN when the
  other resolves, and both treat an empty final addr set as a typed error
  (`NXDomainError`/`NoDataError`), never a silent `([], nil)`. Residual leak
  for both: TLS SNI/timing to the configured provider.

## DNS endpoints and resolver privacy trade-offs

The optional `[dns]` block selects which transport resolves a peer's hostname
endpoint when opted in with `dns = true` per-peer. It is **opt-in by default —
[dns] alone never enables hostname resolution**; every peer endpoint is always
an IP literal unless explicitly marked `dns = true`. Hostname endpoints are
resolved through the OS system resolver by default (when `[dns]` is absent).

### Why default-off: the DPI thesis

A **pre-tunnel hostname lookup** is an unencrypted (cleartext) signal: an
on-path adversary sees the edge asking for the public concentrator's hostname
*before* the tunnel is up, making the host blocklistable at the DNS level
without inspecting any encrypted traffic. This is true regardless of how the
lookup is done — system resolver, DoH/DoT — because the resolver *itself*
learns the query. The default posture (IP literals only; hostnames deferred
to an explicit opt-in with `dns = true` + an explicit `[dns]` resolver block)
keeps this leakage off by default and surfaces it as an intentional choice.

### Leaked artifacts per resolver mode

Once a peer opts into hostname resolution (`dns = true`), the transport choice
in `[dns].resolver` determines what information escapes to a passive on-path
observer — per Requirement-6 testing (test/e2e/p5_dpi_test.go, Q29/Q33):

- **system** (default, OS stub resolver): A **cleartext DNS query naming the
  concentrator's hostname** — the full QNAME in plaintext on port 53. This is
  the most visible artifact: a DPI engine observes the exact hostname being
  resolved and can block it pre-emptively at the network edge.

- **DoH** (DNS-over-HTTPS, RFC 8484): The **TLS ClientHello SNI** (Server Name
  Indication, naming the DoH provider's host) **plus timing/connection metadata**
  to the DoH provider. The query payload itself is encrypted within the HTTPS
  tunnel, but the SNI and the observed request/response cadence allow timing-based
  inference and correlation to the DoH provider — the concentrator hostname is
  *not* visible on the wire.

- **DoT** (DNS-over-TLS, RFC 7858): Identical to DoH: the **TLS ClientHello SNI**
  (naming the DoT server) **plus timing and connection metadata** to the DoT
  provider. The query is encrypted, but SNI + timing correlates the edge to that
  resolver.

In all three cases, **multi-record expansions** (a hostname resolving to multiple
A/AAAA records, e.g. concentrator failover `endpoints` list) feed back into
hub-failover: each address in the result set feeds the active-backup or weighted
scheduler as a separate routable endpoint, so the edge can failover within that
set on liveness loss (one address down → try the next).

### Opt-in defer-and-reconcile boot semantics

A hostname endpoint that cannot be resolved at startup (resolver down, DNS
outage, network unreachable) **never blocks tunnel bring-up**. The tunnel
boots without that endpoint, and a background re-resolution loop (at the
cadence `[dns].poll_interval`, default 30s) installs it and initiates the
Noise handshake on the first successful lookup. Steady-state re-resolution then
repoints the bond whenever the hostname's record changes (TTL-driven or faster,
per the operational `poll_interval`).

- **Re-resolution cadence**: `[dns].poll_interval` (default 30s, must be > 0).
  Governs how often an opted-in hostname endpoint is re-resolved; changes are
  reconciled immediately.

- **Liveness-loss trigger**: If all endpoints (resolved A/AAAA records + any
  IP-literal endpoints) for a peer go unreachable simultaneously, the
  re-resolution loop shortens its cadence and retries aggressively; once any
  endpoint recovers, it returns to the normal cadence.

- **Change suppression**: Re-resolution results that are identical to the prior
  set (same IP + port, same order) do not trigger re-keying or re-handshake —
  they are silent, minimizing control churn.

### Mixing rules with ordered endpoints

When a peer declares multiple endpoints via the `endpoints` list (hub-failover),
and one or more are hostnames (`dns = true`), the following rules apply:

1. **Each hostname expands to its full A+AAAA record set** at resolve time; IP
   literals and resolved addresses are all ordered together per the scheduler's
   active-backup or weighted policy.

2. **Order preservation**: the `endpoints` list order is **strict** — the scheduler
   respects it for active-backup failover (index 0 is primary, index N are standbys).
   When a hostname at index K resolves to multiple addresses, they are inserted
   *consecutively* in that order (first address fills index K, subsequent addresses
   shift later entries right in the ordering).

3. **Deduplication**: if two `endpoints` entries (whether IP or hostname) resolve
   to the same address:port, both are rejected at config load (duplicates not
   allowed in the flattened set).

4. **Resolver selection is global**: `[dns].resolver` applies to *all* opted-in
   hostname endpoints — you cannot mix system + DoH + DoT within one config.
   Choose one transport per tunnel.

## Load-bearing invariants

These are the rules that keep the design correct; break them and the tunnel
misbehaves subtly. Agents and contributors must preserve them.

1. **One virtual endpoint per peer (A1).** The engine sees a single stable
   `Endpoint`; the Bind fans out beneath it. Never surface per-path endpoint
   churn to the engine.
2. **Own outer sequence space.** The resequencer/FEC use wanbond's outer-seq;
   never reuse or perturb the inner WireGuard counter.
3. **Resequence before inner anti-replay.** Restore cross-path order in `reseq`
   before the engine's replay window validates.
4. **Inner fail-closed; outer control authenticated.** WireGuard authenticates the
   payload; PROBE/CONTROL are PSK-HMAC authenticated with monotonic anti-replay;
   DATA/PARITY are deliberately unauthenticated (see Security model).
5. **Amnezia is isolated to `bind.go`.** All engine coupling goes through the type
   aliases there.
6. **Amnezia is all-or-nothing** and **single-engine-per-process** (its magic-
   header logic uses package-level globals). Config validation enforces the
   former; deployments must honor the latter.
7. **Re-verify the reedsolomon prefix invariant** on any FEC-dependency bump.

## Security model

- **Payload**: confidentiality, integrity, authenticity provided by inner
  WireGuard (Noise + AEAD). wanbond never sees plaintext.
- **Outer control plane** (PROBE, CONTROL): PSK-HMAC authenticated + per-peer
  monotonic anti-replay — an attacker cannot forge or replay them.
- **Outer data plane** (DATA, PARITY): **unauthenticated by design**. A network
  attacker can forge/replay these; forgeries are dropped by the inner AEAD (real
  payload) or discarded by the FEC decoder as inconsistent. The accepted residual
  risk is **DoS-grade only** (an attacker can waste decode/resequence work), never
  a confidentiality or integrity break. This trade buys ~0 per-packet overhead on
  the hot path.
- **Traffic analysis / DPI**: the outer wire has no fingerprint (random nonce,
  obfuscated body, no magic bytes); AmneziaWG junk params add defense-in-depth.
  Protocol *mimicry* (looking like HTTPS) is an explicit non-goal.

## Not yet built / deliberate boundaries

These are recorded design boundaries, not defects:

- **No live CONTROL protocol.** The CONTROL frame kind and its `ControlGuard`
  anti-replay exist and are tested, but inbound CONTROL is currently dropped at
  the Bind (`multipath.go` receive default case). It is the chokepoint a future
  out-of-band signalling layer (e.g. explicit rekey/state) must route through.
- **Pacing ships disabled by default; no live auto-tuning (Q20).** Empirical
  sizing *is* built: `SizePacingFromBDP` derives the per-path pace from an
  operator-declared per-link bandwidth (`link_bandwidth`/`link_rtt`) at config
  load (T53), and enabled-pacing was measured to eliminate bufferbloat both on the
  bandwidth-capped netns fixture (T56/T61) and on the real-link tier (T58). What
  stays a **deliberate boundary**: pacing is off unless `[scheduler] pacing_enabled
  = true`, and the declared bandwidth is fixed at load — wanbond never re-derives
  the pace live from runtime measurements (Q20 rejected a live control loop for the
  pilot). Absent a declared bandwidth (or with pacing off) the default per-path
  capacity is synthetic (~115 Mbit/s), well above realistic uplinks.
- **In-fixture throughput/bufferbloat measurement is CPU-bound (a fixture
  boundary).** The netns fixture proves *functional* bonding/FEC/failover/DPI but
  is CPU/PPS-bound, so absolute "bonded ≈ sum of links" throughput and bufferbloat
  are **not** measured there — they are measured on the **real-link tier**. The
  capped-fixture BDP sub-test (T52) and the realhosts tier (`just p0-baseline` →
  `TestRealAggregationBufferbloat` / `TestRealMidTransferWANKill`, T58/T63) record
  the aggregation ratio and loaded-vs-idle RTT **report-only**. Note the realhosts
  topology shares a single physical uplink, so the measured aggregation ratio is
  ~≤1 — this is an informational, report-only measurement, not a bandwidth-
  aggregation guarantee (see
  [manual-checklist.md §P0](manual-checklist.md#p0--automated-real-link-baseline-realhosts-tier)
  and [p0-findings.md](p0-findings.md)).
- **Multi-concentrator hub-failover: UDP-only remains a non-goal.** Q18 brought
  edge-side ORDERED-ENDPOINT ACTIVE-STANDBY hub failover into scope; the config
  surface (T54), the switch (T57), the netns e2e (T62), and the real-link
  mid-transfer WAN-kill tier (T63) are all built and validated (see *Concentrator
  hub failover* above). What stays out of scope: UDP-only is deliberate — there is
  no TCP/TLS fallback for wholesale-UDP-block networks — and the endpoint list is
  IP:port only (no DNS resolution).

## References

- [p0-findings.md](p0-findings.md) — the P0 spike that fixed the single-endpoint,
  resequencing, and fixture-is-CPU-bound decisions.
- [p0-checkpoint.md](p0-checkpoint.md) — phase gate + deferred-work record.
- [install.md](install.md) — deployment and operation.
- [manual-checklist.md](manual-checklist.md) — manual per-phase + real-link
  verification.

active/primary hub, the rest are ordered standbys tried in order:

   ```toml
   [[wireguard.peers]]
   public_key  = "<base64 CONCENTRATOR public key>"
   endpoints   = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]  # [active, standby, ...]
   allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]
   ```

   `endpoints` is **mutually exclusive** with `endpoint`; the single `endpoint`
   form is just its one-element case and takes **no** failover action, so a
   one-concentrator deployment stays exactly as in step 2. Endpoints are
   **IP:port only — no DNS resolution**.

2. **The standby shares the active's WireGuard static key.** All hubs in the
   list present the **same** WireGuard identity, so the edge re-handshakes to
   whichever hub is active against the single `public_key` it configured. In
   practice the standby's `concentrator.toml` is a **clone** of the active's —
   *same* `[wireguard].private_key`, same `listen_port`, same edge peer block —
   deployed on the standby's public IP:

150	  eliminate bufferbloat on the bandwidth-capped netns fixture and the real-link
151	  tier. wanbond fixes the pace at config load and does not auto-tune it live (Q20).
152	- **Throughput aggregation and bufferbloat are not measured by the netns fixture**
153	  (it is CPU-bound) — the report-only real-link tier (`just p0-baseline`) measures
154	  them instead; validate on your own uplinks before a production rollout.
155	- **No live CONTROL protocol** — the frame type and its anti-replay guard exist,
156	  but inbound CONTROL is currently dropped (reserved for future signalling).
157	- **Multi-concentrator hub-failover: built and validated** — an
158	  edge peer may declare an ORDERED `endpoints` list (active concentrator + ordered
159	  standbys); the single `endpoint` form still works unchanged (its one-element
160	  case, which takes no failover action). On HUB LOSS (every path to the active
161	  concentrator down at once) the edge advances to the next endpoint, repoints the
162	  bond, and re-handshakes a fresh session (round-robin/wrap at end of list). The
163	  switch is covered by unit/component tests, the netns hub-failover e2e (T62), and
164	  the real-link mid-transfer WAN-kill tier (T63). Endpoints may be IP:port
165	  literals (default) or hostnames with opt-in `dns = true` per-peer and an
166	  explicit `[dns]` resolver block; see
167	  [docs/design.md §DNS endpoints and resolver privacy trade-offs](docs/design.md).
168	- **UDP only** — obfuscation defeats DPI *classification*, not a wholesale UDP
169	  block; there is no TCP/TLS fallback.
170	- **DATA/PARITY frames are unauthenticated by design** (inner WireGuard
171	  authenticates the real payload; a DoS-grade forgery risk is accepted). PROBE
172	  and CONTROL are PSK-HMAC authenticated.
173	
174	See [docs/design.md §Security model](docs/design.md) and

file.content:
eliminate bufferbloat on the bandwidth-capped netns fixture and the real-link
  tier. wanbond fixes the pace at config load and does not auto-tune it live (Q20).
- **Throughput aggregation and bufferbloat are not measured by the netns fixture**
  (it is CPU-bound) — the report-only real-link tier (`just p0-baseline`) measures
  them instead; validate on your own uplinks before a production rollout.
- **No live CONTROL protocol** — the frame type and its anti-replay guard exist,
  but inbound CONTROL is currently dropped (reserved for future signalling).
- **Multi-concentrator hub-failover: built and validated** — an
  edge peer may declare an ORDERED `endpoints` list (active concentrator + ordered
  standbys); the single `endpoint` form still works unchanged (its one-element
  case, which takes no failover action). On HUB LOSS (every path to the active
  concentrator down at once) the edge advances to the next endpoint, repoints the
  bond, and re-handshakes a fresh session (round-robin/wrap at end of list). The
  switch is covered by unit/component tests, the netns hub-failover e2e (T62), and
  the real-link mid-transfer WAN-kill tier (T63). Endpoints may be IP:port
  literals (default) or hostnames with opt-in `dns = true` per-peer and an
  explicit `[dns]` resolver block; see
  [docs/design.md §DNS endpoints and resolver privacy trade-offs](docs/design.md).
- **UDP only** — obfuscation defeats DPI *classification*, not a wholesale UDP
  block; there is no TCP/TLS fallback.
- **DATA/PARITY frames are unauthenticated by design** (inner WireGuard
  authenticates the real payload; a DoS-grade forgery risk is accepted). PROBE
  and CONTROL are PSK-HMAC authenticated.

package config

import (
	"encoding/base64"
	"net/netip"
	"os"
	"path/filepath"
	"reflect"
	"strings"
	"testing"
)

// testKey returns a syntactically valid base64 32-byte key for fixtures.
func testKey(b byte) string {
	raw := make([]byte, keyLen)
	for i := range raw {
		raw[i] = b
	}
	return base64.StdEncoding.EncodeToString(raw)
}

// writeConfig writes body to a temp file with the given mode and returns its path.
func writeConfig(t *testing.T, mode os.FileMode, body string) string {
	t.Helper()
	dir := t.TempDir()
	path := filepath.Join(dir, "wanbond.toml")
	if err := os.WriteFile(path, []byte(body), mode); err != nil {
		t.Fatalf("write fixture: %v", err)
	}
	// os.WriteFile is subject to umask; force the exact bits.
	if err := os.Chmod(path, mode); err != nil {
		t.Fatalf("chmod fixture: %v", err)
	}
	return path
}

// Root-level scalars (role, psk) must precede any TOML table header.
const edgeConfig = `
role = "edge"
psk = "%PSK%"

[[paths]]
name = "starlink"
source_addr = "192.0.2.10"

[[paths]]
name = "cellular"
source_addr = "192.0.2.20"

[wireguard]
private_key = "%PRIV%"
[[wireguard.peers]]
public_key = "%PUB%"
endpoint = "203.0.113.5:51820"
allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]

[amnezia]
jc = 4
jmin = 8
jmax = 80
s1 = 15
s2 = 92

[metrics]
listen = "127.0.0.1:9095"

[log]
level = "info"
`

const concentratorConfig = `
role = "concentrator"
psk = "%PSK%"

[[paths]]
name = "wan"
source_addr = "203.0.113.5"

[wireguard]
private_key = "%PRIV%"
listen_port = 51820
[[wireguard.peers]]
public_key = "%PUB%"
allowed_ips = ["10.0.0.2/32"]
`

func fill(tmpl string) string {
	r := strings.NewReplacer("%PRIV%", testKey(1), "%PUB%", testKey(2), "%PSK%", testKey(3))
	return r.Replace(tmpl)
}

func TestLoadValidEdge(t *testing.T) {
	path := writeConfig(t, 0o600, fill(edgeConfig))
	c, err := Load(path)
	if err != nil {
		t.Fatalf("Load: %v", err)
	}
	if c.Role != RoleEdge {
		t.Errorf("role = %q, want edge", c.Role)
	}

1	# wanbond.example.toml — exhaustive annotated configuration reference.
2	#
3	# Every key wanbond reads is present here. Copy this file, fill the <placeholders>,
4	# and delete what you do not need:
5	#
6	#   sudo install -m 0600 /dev/null /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
7	#   $EDITOR /etc/wanbond/edge.toml     # paste + edit this content
8	#
9	# 0600 is MANDATORY: the file holds the WireGuard private key and the PSK, so
10	# config.Load refuses any file whose mode is not exactly 0600.
11	#
12	# This example is EDGE-oriented. For a CONCENTRATOR: set role = "concentrator",
13	# uncomment [wireguard].listen_port, and REMOVE the peer `endpoint`/`endpoints`
14	# (the concentrator learns the edge's endpoints from authenticated traffic).
15	#
16	# Required keys are shown live; optional keys are commented-out with their
17	# DEFAULT value (uncommenting one at its shown value is a no-op). Cross-field
18	# constraints a single example cannot express inline are listed at the bottom.
19	# Full prose reference: docs/install.md §3z. Everything here is enforced at
20	# config load (fail-fast on the first violation) except the loopback + allowed_ips
21	# checks noted below.
22	
23	# ── top level ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
24	role = "edge"                      # REQUIRED. "edge" | "concentrator". Never
25	                                   #   inferred from other keys.
26	psk  = "<base64 32-byte PSK>"      # REQUIRED. 32 raw bytes, base64. SAME value on
27	                                   #   both ends; keys the PSK-HMAC that
28	                                   #   authenticates outer PROBE/CONTROL frames.
29	                                   #   Generate: head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64
30	# tun_persist = false              # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT false. When false the TUN
31	                                   #   wanbond0 is destroyed on daemon stop, so its
32	                                   #   addresses/routes/rules are dropped on every
33	                                   #   restart (today's teardown semantics). Set
34	                                   #   true and the daemon issues TUNSETPERSIST on
35	                                   #   start: wanbond0 SURVIVES a daemon restart
36	                                   #   with the SAME ifindex, so operator-owned
37	                                   #   addressing persists untouched. NOTE (D39): a
38	                                   #   persistent device STILL needs the
39	                                   #   NetworkManager unmanaged-devices drop-in on
40	                                   #   NM hosts — persistence keeps the link, it
41	                                   #   does not exempt it from NM.
42	
43	# ── paths: one [[paths]] block per WAN uplink; at least one is REQUIRED ───────
44	[[paths]]
45	name        = "starlink"           # REQUIRED. Stable, unique identifier.
46	source_addr = "192.168.1.10"       # REQUIRED. Bare local source IP the path's UDP
47	                                   #   socket binds to. Must be unique across paths
48	                                   #   (a shared source collides EADDRINUSE at the
49	                                   #   second bind). No default.
50	# dest_addr = "203.0.113.7:51820"  # OPTIONAL, edge-only meaning. Per-path
51	                                   #   concentrator endpoint (ip:port). Omit when
52	                                   #   one public IP fronts all uplinks (the peer's
53	                                   #   endpoint is reused). No default. Inert on the
54	                                   #   concentrator (it learns edge endpoints).
55	# link_bandwidth = "50Mbit"        # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared bottleneck
56	                                   #   bandwidth. SI bit/s: k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9
57	                                   #   ("bit" may be written "bps"). Must be > 0.
58	                                   #   Used ONLY under weighted policy + pacing;
59	                                   #   inert otherwise. No default (undeclared).
60	# link_rtt = "45ms"                # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared baseline RTT (Go
61	                                   #   duration). REQUIRED (> 0) when link_bandwidth
62	                                   #   is set under weighted pacing; else ignored.
63	
64	# A second uplink (edge). Repeat the block per path.
65	[[paths]]
66	name        = "5g"
67	source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
68	
69	# ── wireguard: inner tunnel key material ─────────────────────────────────────
70	[wireguard]
71	private_key = "<base64 32-byte private key>"  # REQUIRED (both roles).
72	                                   #   Generate: wg genkey (public: … | wg pubkey)
73	# listen_port = 51820              # CONCENTRATOR-ONLY: REQUIRED (> 0) there; omit /
74	                                   #   leave 0 on the edge. uint16 (< 1024 needs
75	                                   #   CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE).
76	
77	# ── wireguard peers: at least one [[wireguard.peers]] is REQUIRED ─────────────
78	[[wireguard.peers]]
79	public_key = "<base64 peer public key>"  # REQUIRED. base64 32-byte key (the OTHER
80	                                   #   end's public key).
81	endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"     # EDGE: REQUIRED (this OR `endpoints`). ip:port
82	                                   #   literal (default, no DNS) OR hostname with
83	                                   #   opt-in `dns = true` below + an explicit [dns]
84	                                   #   resolver block. CONCENTRATOR: must be ABSENT
85	                                   #   (rejected) — it roams the edge dynamically.
86	                                   #   Mutually exclusive with `endpoints`.
87	# dns = false                       # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT false. Set true to enable
88	                                   #   hostname resolution for the endpoint above
89	                                   #   (requires an explicit [dns] resolver block).
90	# endpoints = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]
91	                                   # EDGE-ONLY ordered hub-failover list: [0] is the
92	                                   #   active concentrator, the rest ordered
93	                                   #   standbys. Each may be an ip:port literal
94	                                   #   (default) or a hostname with dns = true; when
95	                                   #   hostnames are used, each resolves to its full
96	                                   #   A+AAAA set and all addresses are ordered
97	                                   #   together in the failover list. duplicates
98	                                   #   rejected. Mutually exclusive with `endpoint`
99	                                   #   (its one-element form). Rejected on the
100	                                   #   concentrator.

# wanbond.example.toml — exhaustive annotated configuration reference.
#
# Every key wanbond reads is present here. Copy this file, fill the <placeholders>,
# and delete what you do not need:
#
#   sudo install -m 0600 /dev/null /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
#   $EDITOR /etc/wanbond/edge.toml     # paste + edit this content
#
# 0600 is MANDATORY: the file holds the WireGuard private key and the PSK, so
# config.Load refuses any file whose mode is not exactly 0600.
#
# This example is EDGE-oriented. For a CONCENTRATOR: set role = "concentrator",
# uncomment [wireguard].listen_port, and REMOVE the peer `endpoint`/`endpoints`
# (the concentrator learns the edge's endpoints from authenticated traffic).
#
# Required keys are shown live; optional keys are commented-out with their
# DEFAULT value (uncommenting one at its shown value is a no-op). Cross-field
# constraints a single example cannot express inline are listed at the bottom.
# Full prose reference: docs/install.md §3z. Everything here is enforced at
# config load (fail-fast on the first violation) except the loopback + allowed_ips
# checks noted below.

# ── top level ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
role = "edge"                      # REQUIRED. "edge" | "concentrator". Never
                                   #   inferred from other keys.
psk  = "<base64 32-byte PSK>"      # REQUIRED. 32 raw bytes, base64. SAME value on
                                   #   both ends; keys the PSK-HMAC that
                                   #   authenticates outer PROBE/CONTROL frames.
                                   #   Generate: head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64
# tun_persist = false              # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT false. When false the TUN
                                   #   wanbond0 is destroyed on daemon stop, so its
                                   #   addresses/routes/rules are dropped on every
                                   #   restart (today's teardown semantics). Set
                                   #   true and the daemon issues TUNSETPERSIST on
                                   #   start: wanbond0 SURVIVES a daemon restart
                                   #   with the SAME ifindex, so operator-owned
                                   #   addressing persists untouched. NOTE (D39): a
                                   #   persistent device STILL needs the
                                   #   NetworkManager unmanaged-devices drop-in on
                                   #   NM hosts — persistence keeps the link, it
                                   #   does not exempt it from NM.

# ── paths: one [[paths]] block per WAN uplink; at least one is REQUIRED ───────
[[paths]]
name        = "starlink"           # REQUIRED. Stable, unique identifier.
source_addr = "192.168.1.10"       # REQUIRED. Bare local source IP the path's UDP
                                   #   socket binds to. Must be unique across paths
                                   #   (a shared source collides EADDRINUSE at the
                                   #   second bind). No default.
# dest_addr = "203.0.113.7:51820"  # OPTIONAL, edge-only meaning. Per-path
                                   #   concentrator endpoint (ip:port). Omit when
                                   #   one public IP fronts all uplinks (the peer's
                                   #   endpoint is reused). No default. Inert on the
                                   #   concentrator (it learns edge endpoints).
# link_bandwidth = "50Mbit"        # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared bottleneck
                                   #   bandwidth. SI bit/s: k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9
                                   #   ("bit" may be written "bps"). Must be > 0.
                                   #   Used ONLY under weighted policy + pacing;
                                   #   inert otherwise. No default (undeclared).
# link_rtt = "45ms"                # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared baseline RTT (Go
                                   #   duration). REQUIRED (> 0) when link_bandwidth
                                   #   is set under weighted pacing; else ignored.

# A second uplink (edge). Repeat the block per path.
[[paths]]
name        = "5g"
source_addr = "192.168.2.10"

# ── wireguard: inner tunnel key material ─────────────────────────────────────
[wireguard]
private_key = "<base64 32-byte private key>"  # REQUIRED (both roles).
                                   #   Generate: wg genkey (public: … | wg pubkey)
# listen_port = 51820              # CONCENTRATOR-ONLY: REQUIRED (> 0) there; omit /
                                   #   leave 0 on the edge. uint16 (< 1024 needs
                                   #   CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE).

# ── wireguard peers: at least one [[wireguard.peers]] is REQUIRED ─────────────
[[wireguard.peers]]
public_key = "<base64 peer public key>"  # REQUIRED. base64 32-byte key (the OTHER
                                   #   end's public key).
endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"     # EDGE: REQUIRED (this OR `endpoints`). ip:port
                                   #   literal (default, no DNS) OR hostname with
                                   #   opt-in `dns = true` below + an explicit [dns]
                                   #   resolver block. CONCENTRATOR: must be ABSENT
                                   #   (rejected) — it roams the edge dynamically.
                                   #   Mutually exclusive with `endpoints`.
# dns = false                       # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT false. Set true to enable
                                   #   hostname resolution for the endpoint above
                                   #   (requires an explicit [dns] resolver block).
# endpoints = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]
                                   # EDGE-ONLY ordered hub-failover list: [0] is the
                                   #   active concentrator, the rest ordered
                                   #   standbys. Each may be an ip:port literal
                                   #   (default) or a hostname with dns = true; when
                                   #   hostnames are used, each resolves to its full
                                   #   A+AAAA set and all addresses are ordered
                                   #   together in the failover list. duplicates
                                   #   rejected. Mutually exclusive with `endpoint`
                                   #   (its one-element form). Rejected on the
                                   #   concentrator.
