1	# wanbond.example.toml — exhaustive annotated configuration reference.
2	#
3	# Every key wanbond reads is present here. Copy this file, fill the <placeholders>,
4	# and delete what you do not need:
5	#
6	#   sudo install -m 0600 /dev/null /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
7	#   $EDITOR /etc/wanbond/edge.toml     # paste + edit this content
8	#
9	# 0600 is MANDATORY: the file holds the WireGuard private key and the PSK, so
10	# config.Load refuses any file whose mode is not exactly 0600.
11	#
12	# This example is EDGE-oriented. For a CONCENTRATOR: set role = "concentrator",
13	# uncomment [wireguard].listen_port, and REMOVE the peer `endpoint`/`endpoints`
14	# (the concentrator learns the edge's endpoints from authenticated traffic).
15	#
16	# Required keys are shown live; optional keys are commented-out with their
17	# DEFAULT value (uncommenting one at its shown value is a no-op). Cross-field
18	# constraints a single example cannot express inline are listed at the bottom.
19	# Full prose reference: docs/install.md §3z. Everything here is enforced at
20	# config load (fail-fast on the first violation) except the loopback + allowed_ips
21	# checks noted below.
22	
23	# ── top level ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
24	role = "edge"                      # REQUIRED. "edge" | "concentrator". Never
25	                                   #   inferred from other keys.
26	psk  = "<base64 32-byte PSK>"      # REQUIRED. 32 raw bytes, base64. SAME value on
27	                                   #   both ends; keys the PSK-HMAC that
28	                                   #   authenticates outer PROBE/CONTROL frames.
29	                                   #   Generate: head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64
30	# tun_persist = false              # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT false. When false the TUN
31	                                   #   wanbond0 is destroyed on daemon stop, so its
32	                                   #   addresses/routes/rules are dropped on every
33	                                   #   restart (today's teardown semantics). Set
34	                                   #   true and the daemon issues TUNSETPERSIST on
35	                                   #   start: wanbond0 SURVIVES a daemon restart
36	                                   #   with the SAME ifindex, so operator-owned
37	                                   #   addressing persists untouched. NOTE (D39): a
38	                                   #   persistent device STILL needs the
39	                                   #   NetworkManager unmanaged-devices drop-in on
40	                                   #   NM hosts — persistence keeps the link, it
41	                                   #   does not exempt it from NM.
42	
43	# ── paths: one [[paths]] block per WAN uplink; at least one is REQUIRED ───────
44	[[paths]]
45	name        = "starlink"           # REQUIRED. Stable, unique identifier.
46	source_addr = "192.168.1.10"       # REQUIRED. Bare local source IP the path's UDP
47	                                   #   socket binds to. Must be unique across paths
48	                                   #   (a shared source collides EADDRINUSE at the
49	                                   #   second bind). No default.
50	# dest_addr = "203.0.113.7:51820"  # OPTIONAL, edge-only meaning. Per-path
51	                                   #   concentrator endpoint (ip:port). Omit when
52	                                   #   one public IP fronts all uplinks (the peer's
53	                                   #   endpoint is reused). No default. Inert on the
54	                                   #   concentrator (it learns edge endpoints).
55	# link_bandwidth = "50Mbit"        # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared bottleneck
56	                                   #   bandwidth. SI bit/s: k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9
57	                                   #   ("bit" may be written "bps"). Must be > 0.
58	                                   #   Used ONLY under weighted policy + pacing;
59	                                   #   inert otherwise. No default (undeclared).
60	# link_rtt = "45ms"                # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared baseline RTT (Go
61	                                   #   duration). REQUIRED (> 0) when link_bandwidth
62	                                   #   is set under weighted pacing; else ignored.
63	
64	# A second uplink (edge). Repeat the block per path.
65	[[paths]]
66	name        = "5g"
67	source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
68	
69	# ── wireguard: inner tunnel key material ─────────────────────────────────────
70	[wireguard]
71	private_key = "<base64 32-byte private key>"  # REQUIRED (both roles).
72	                                   #   Generate: wg genkey (public: … | wg pubkey)
73	# listen_port = 51820              # CONCENTRATOR-ONLY: REQUIRED (> 0) there; omit /
74	                                   #   leave 0 on the edge. uint16 (< 1024 needs
75	                                   #   CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE).
76	
77	# ── wireguard peers: at least one [[wireguard.peers]] is REQUIRED ─────────────
78	[[wireguard.peers]]
79	public_key = "<base64 peer public key>"  # REQUIRED. base64 32-byte key (the OTHER
80	                                   #   end's public key).
81	endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"     # EDGE: REQUIRED (this OR `endpoints`). ip:port
82	                                   #   literal (default, no DNS) OR hostname with
83	                                   #   opt-in `dns = true` below + an explicit [dns]
84	                                   #   resolver block. CONCENTRATOR: must be ABSENT
85	                                   #   (rejected) — it roams the edge dynamically.
86	                                   #   Mutually exclusive with `endpoints`.
87	# dns = false                       # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT false. Set true to enable
88	                                   #   hostname resolution for the endpoint above
89	                                   #   (requires an explicit [dns] resolver block).
90	# endpoints = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]
91	                                   # EDGE-ONLY ordered hub-failover list: [0] is the
92	                                   #   active concentrator, the rest ordered
93	                                   #   standbys. Each may be an ip:port literal
94	                                   #   (default) or a hostname with dns = true; when
95	                                   #   hostnames are used, each resolves to its full
96	                                   #   A+AAAA set and all addresses are ordered
97	                                   #   together in the failover list. duplicates
98	                                   #   rejected. Mutually exclusive with `endpoint`
99	                                   #   (its one-element form). Rejected on the
100	                                   #   concentrator.
101	allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]     # REQUIRED: >= 1 CIDR routed to this peer
102	                                   #   (enforced when the WG UAPI is built). This is
103	                                   #   the peer's inner tunnel address(es). A literal
104	                                   #   0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0 (full tunnel) is ALWAYS split
105	                                   #   into the equivalent /1+/1 pair at UAPI render —
106	                                   #   the engine never sees the literal /0.
107	# mode = "default-route"           # OPTIONAL, edge-only. Marks this peer as the
108	                                   #   edge's full-tunnel concentrator (opts into a
109	                                   #   0.0.0.0/0 / ::/0 allowed_ips entry above).
110	                                   #   Rejected on the concentrator. Config-surface
111	                                   #   only today: it does not itself install any
112	                                   #   OS-level default route.
113	
114	# ── second peer example (hostname endpoint with DNS opt-in) ──────────────────────
115	# [[wireguard.peers]]
116	# public_key = "<base64 peer public key>"
117	# endpoint = "concentrator.example.com:51820"  # hostname endpoint (requires dns = true)
118	# dns = true                        # enable hostname resolution for this endpoint
119	#                                   #   (also requires an explicit [dns] resolver block)
120	# allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]
121	
122	# ── amnezia obfuscation: OPTIONAL, OFF by default (plain WireGuard) ───────────
123	# ALL-OR-NOTHING: either omit the whole block, or set the entire jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2
124	# set — IDENTICALLY on both ends. One obfuscation engine per process.
125	# [amnezia]
126	# jc   = 4                         # junk packet count. > 0 when configured.
127	# jmin = 40                        # min junk packet size. > 0; jmin <= jmax.
128	# jmax = 70                        # max junk packet size. > 0.
129	# s1   = 30                        # init-packet junk prefix size. > 0.
130	# s2   = 40                        # response-packet junk prefix size. > 0.
131	                                   #   Constraint: (148 + s1) != (92 + s2) — the
132	                                   #   obfuscated init/response lengths must differ.
133	                                   #   (no defaults: required when the block present.)
134	# h1 = 1                           # magic header: initiation. Default 1..4 when the
135	# h2 = 2                           #   block is configured but NO header is given.
136	# h3 = 3                           #   When any is set, h1..h4 must be a DISTINCT set
137	# h4 = 4                           #   (values <= 4 mean "standard message type").
138	
139	# ── scheduler: OPTIONAL. Omitted => active-backup, all knobs below ignored ────
140	# [scheduler]
141	# policy = "active-backup"         # "active-backup" (DEFAULT) | "weighted". Every
142	                                   #   knob below applies ONLY to "weighted"; under
143	                                   #   active-backup they are inert and left unset.
144	# per_path_capacity_fps = 10000.0  # DEFAULT 10000. Reference per-path capacity
145	                                   #   (frame slots/s): aggregation-gate denominator
146	                                   #   and pacing refill rate. > 0.
147	# engage_fraction    = 0.9         # DEFAULT 0.9. Engage aggregation above
148	                                   #   engage_fraction * capacity. In (0, 1].
149	# disengage_fraction = 0.5         # DEFAULT 0.5. Collapse below disengage_fraction
150	                                   #   * capacity. Must be in [0, engage_fraction) —
151	                                   #   the hysteresis band.
152	# collapse_dwell = "2s"            # DEFAULT 2s. Sustained-low dwell before
153	                                   #   collapsing to primary-only. >= 0.
154	# load_tau = "200ms"               # DEFAULT 200ms. Offered-load rate estimator time
155	                                   #   constant. > 0.
156	# pacing_enabled = false           # DEFAULT false. Turn on per-path send-pacing
157	                                   #   (token buckets). Off => buckets bypassed.
158	# pacing_burst_frames = 64.0       # DEFAULT 64. Token-bucket burst (frames). > 0
159	                                   #   when pacing_enabled.
160	# weight_rtt_floor = "1ms"         # DEFAULT 1ms. RTT floor in the weight formula. >0.
161	# weight_loss_floor = 0.001        # DEFAULT 1e-3. Loss floor under the sqrt in the
162	                                   #   weight formula. > 0.
163	
164	# ── fec: OPTIONAL, OFF by default (no parity on the wire) ─────────────────────
165	# [fec]
166	# enabled = true                   # DEFAULT false. Turns the FEC plane on; when
167	                                   #   false every field below is ignored.
168	# data_shards = 10                 # K: inner datagrams per coding group. >= 1 when
169	                                   #   enabled. No default (required).
170	# parity_shards = 6                # M: parity frames per group (and the adaptive
171	                                   #   parity CEILING). >= 1 when enabled; K + M <=
172	                                   #   256. No default (required).
173	# deadline = "5ms"                 # DEFAULT 5ms. Partial-group flush deadline. > 0
174	                                   #   and <= 125ms.
175	# adaptive = false                 # DEFAULT false. Closed-loop parity controller
176	                                   #   (parity tracks measured loss up to the
177	                                   #   ceiling). Requires enabled = true.
178	# target_residual = 0.005          # ADAPTIVE-ONLY, primary sizing surface:
179	                                   #   post-recovery residual-loss SLA in (0, 1). No
180	                                   #   default. Mutually exclusive with safety_factor.
181	# safety_factor = 1.5              # ADAPTIVE-ONLY, legacy headroom multiplier >= 1.
182	                                   #   DEFAULTS to 1.5 when adaptive and NEITHER field
183	                                   #   is set. Must stay 0 in fixed mode. Mutually
184	                                   #   exclusive with target_residual.
185	
186	# ── dns: OPTIONAL. Omitted => system resolver, default cadence/timeouts ───────
187	# Selects the TRANSPORT a peer's opt-in hostname endpoint (dns = true, above)
188	# is resolved through. [dns] alone never turns hostname resolution on.
189	#
190	# EXAMPLE 1 (IP-literal DoH resolver):
191	# [dns]
192	# resolver = "doh"                 # "system" | "doh" | "dot"
193	# doh_url = "https://198.51.100.1/dns-query"  # REQUIRED iff resolver = "doh"
194	# poll_interval = "30s"            # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT 30s. Re-resolution cadence.
195	# timeout = "5s"                   # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT 5s. Per-lookup timeout.
196	#
197	# EXAMPLE 2 (hostname DoT resolver, requires bootstrap_ip):
198	# [dns]
199	# resolver = "dot"                 # "system" | "doh" | "dot"
200	# dot_server = "dns.example.com:853"  # REQUIRED iff resolver = "dot"
201	# bootstrap_ip = "198.51.100.2"    # REQUIRED: dot_server's host is a hostname
202	                                   #   (BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant: must reach the
203	                                   #   resolver WITHOUT DNS lookup itself).
204	# poll_interval = "30s"
205	# timeout = "5s"
206	#
207	# EXAMPLE 3 (system resolver, any IP-literal endpoint):
208	# [dns]
209	# resolver = "system"              # DEFAULT "system". No URL/server/bootstrap needed.
210	# poll_interval = "30s"
211	# timeout = "5s"
212	#
213	# FULL [dns] BLOCK (EXAMPLE 1 — IP-literal DoH):
214	# [dns]
215	# resolver = "doh"                 # "system" (default) | "doh" | "dot".
216	# doh_url = "https://198.51.100.1/dns-query"  # REQUIRED iff resolver = "doh"
217	                                   #   Must be https://.
218	# dot_server = "198.51.100.1"      # REQUIRED iff resolver = "dot"; rejected
219	                                   #   under any other resolver. Bare host, or
220	                                   #   "host:port" where port must be 853 (the
221	                                   #   IANA-assigned DoT port).
222	# bootstrap_ip = "198.51.100.1"    # REQUIRED iff doh_url/dot_server's host is a
223	                                   #   hostname rather than an IP literal
224	                                   #   (BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant: the private
225	                                   #   resolver's own address must be reachable
226	                                   #   WITHOUT a DNS lookup). REJECTED (must stay
227	                                   #   absent) when the host is already an IP
228	                                   #   literal. No default.
229	# poll_interval = "30s"            # DEFAULT 30s. Re-resolution cadence for an
230	                                   #   opted-in hostname peer endpoint. > 0.
231	# timeout = "5s"                   # DEFAULT 5s. Per-lookup bound. > 0.
232	
233	# ── metrics: OPTIONAL. Omit the block => no /metrics endpoint is served ───────
234	[metrics]
235	listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"          # No default. LOOPBACK-ONLY (127.0.0.0/8, ::1, or
236	                                   #   a hostname resolving only to loopback); any
237	                                   #   other address is REFUSED when the endpoint
238	                                   #   binds (not at load).
239	
240	# ── log: OPTIONAL ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
241	[log]
242	level = "info"                     # DEFAULT "info" (empty => info). One of
243	                                   #   debug | info | warn | error ("warning"
244	                                   #   accepted for warn). Unknown => fail-fast.
245	
246	# ── Cross-field constraints (not expressible inline) ─────────────────────────
247	# • Roles: `role` is required, never inferred. Edge-only: per-peer endpoint/
248	#   endpoints (required on edge, rejected on concentrator) + the pacing
249	#   declarations (dest_addr is edge-only in effect, merely inert on the
250	#   concentrator). Concentrator-only: wireguard.listen_port (required there).
251	# • endpoint vs endpoints: mutually exclusive; `endpoint` is the one-element form
252	#   of `endpoints`. On the edge exactly one must be present; the concentrator sets
253	#   neither. Entries are ip:port literals only (no DNS), de-duplicated.
254	# • Distinct source_addr: each [[paths]] needs a unique, valid source_addr
255	#   (compared unmapped, so 192.0.2.10 and ::ffff:192.0.2.10 collide). `name` must
256	#   also be unique.
257	# • allowed_ips: >= 1 CIDR per peer; empty is rejected when the WG config is
258	#   assembled (not by config.validate).
259	# • amnezia all-or-nothing: an unconfigured (all-zero) block is plain WireGuard.
260	#   Once ANY of jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2/h1..h4 is set, the full jc,jmin,jmax,s1,s2 set
261	#   must be > 0, jmin <= jmax, 148+s1 != 92+s2, and h1..h4 must be a distinct set
262	#   (omitted headers default to 1..4). Same profile on both ends. One engine/process.
263	# • link_bandwidth + link_rtt are a pair: both-or-neither, and all-or-nothing
264	#   across every path (declare on every [[paths]] block or none). They take effect
265	#   ONLY under scheduler.policy = "weighted" with pacing_enabled = true; otherwise
266	#   a declared bandwidth is inert. When active they are mutually exclusive with the
267	#   raw per_path_capacity_fps / pacing_burst_frames knobs.
268	# • scheduler off-unless-present: no [scheduler] => active-backup, every weighted
269	#   knob ignored. Under weighted, the hysteresis band requires
270	#   disengage_fraction < engage_fraction.
271	# • fec off-unless-present: no [fec] (or enabled = false) => no parity. adaptive =
272	#   true requires enabled = true. In adaptive mode set EITHER target_residual OR
273	#   safety_factor, never both; both must stay 0 (unset) in fixed mode.
274	# • metrics loopback-only: listen must be a loopback address (or a hostname
275	#   resolving entirely to loopback); a non-loopback bind is refused when the
276	#   endpoint starts. Omit the block to serve no metrics.
277	# • dns off-unless-present: no [dns] => system resolver, default cadence/
278	#   timeouts; [dns] only selects the transport a peer's `dns = true` opt-in
279	#   uses, never a gate by itself. doh_url/dot_server are mode-specific: set
280	#   exactly the one matching `resolver`, the other (and bootstrap_ip under
281	#   resolver = "system") must stay absent. BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant: a
282	#   hostname-form doh_url/dot_server host REQUIRES bootstrap_ip; an IP
283	#   literal host REJECTS a non-empty bootstrap_ip (mode mismatch).
284

file.content:
# wanbond.example.toml — exhaustive annotated configuration reference.
#
# Every key wanbond reads is present here. Copy this file, fill the <placeholders>,
# and delete what you do not need:
#
#   sudo install -m 0600 /dev/null /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
#   $EDITOR /etc/wanbond/edge.toml     # paste + edit this content
#
# 0600 is MANDATORY: the file holds the WireGuard private key and the PSK, so
# config.Load refuses any file whose mode is not exactly 0600.
#
# This example is EDGE-oriented. For a CONCENTRATOR: set role = "concentrator",
# uncomment [wireguard].listen_port, and REMOVE the peer `endpoint`/`endpoints`
# (the concentrator learns the edge's endpoints from authenticated traffic).
#
# Required keys are shown live; optional keys are commented-out with their
# DEFAULT value (uncommenting one at its shown value is a no-op). Cross-field
# constraints a single example cannot express inline are listed at the bottom.
# Full prose reference: docs/install.md §3z. Everything here is enforced at
# config load (fail-fast on the first violation) except the loopback + allowed_ips
# checks noted below.

# ── top level ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
role = "edge"                      # REQUIRED. "edge" | "concentrator". Never
                                   #   inferred from other keys.
psk  = "<base64 32-byte PSK>"      # REQUIRED. 32 raw bytes, base64. SAME value on
                                   #   both ends; keys the PSK-HMAC that
                                   #   authenticates outer PROBE/CONTROL frames.
                                   #   Generate: head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64
# tun_persist = false              # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT false. When false the TUN
                                   #   wanbond0 is destroyed on daemon stop, so its
                                   #   addresses/routes/rules are dropped on every
                                   #   restart (today's teardown semantics). Set
                                   #   true and the daemon issues TUNSETPERSIST on
                                   #   start: wanbond0 SURVIVES a daemon restart
                                   #   with the SAME ifindex, so operator-owned
                                   #   addressing persists untouched. NOTE (D39): a
                                   #   persistent device STILL needs the
                                   #   NetworkManager unmanaged-devices drop-in on
                                   #   NM hosts — persistence keeps the link, it
                                   #   does not exempt it from NM.

# ── paths: one [[paths]] block per WAN uplink; at least one is REQUIRED ───────
[[paths]]
name        = "starlink"           # REQUIRED. Stable, unique identifier.
source_addr = "192.168.1.10"       # REQUIRED. Bare local source IP the path's UDP
                                   #   socket binds to. Must be unique across paths
                                   #   (a shared source collides EADDRINUSE at the
                                   #   second bind). No default.
# dest_addr = "203.0.113.7:51820"  # OPTIONAL, edge-only meaning. Per-path
                                   #   concentrator endpoint (ip:port). Omit when
                                   #   one public IP fronts all uplinks (the peer's
                                   #   endpoint is reused). No default. Inert on the
                                   #   concentrator (it learns edge endpoints).
# link_bandwidth = "50Mbit"        # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared bottleneck
                                   #   bandwidth. SI bit/s: k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9
                                   #   ("bit" may be written "bps"). Must be > 0.
                                   #   Used ONLY under weighted policy + pacing;
                                   #   inert otherwise. No default (undeclared).
# link_rtt = "45ms"                # OPTIONAL. Operator-declared baseline RTT (Go
                                   #   duration). REQUIRED (> 0) when link_bandwidth
                                   #   is set under weighted pacing; else ignored.

# A second uplink (edge). Repeat the block per path.
[[paths]]
name        = "5g"
source_addr = "192.168.2.10"

# ── wireguard: inner tunnel key material ─────────────────────────────────────
[wireguard]
private_key = "<base64 32-byte private key>"  # REQUIRED (both roles).
                                   #   Generate: wg genkey (public: … | wg pubkey)
# listen_port = 51820              # CONCENTRATOR-ONLY: REQUIRED (> 0) there; omit /
                                   #   leave 0 on the edge. uint16 (< 1024 needs
                                   #   CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE).

# ── wireguard peers: at least one [[wireguard.peers]] is REQUIRED ─────────────
[[wireguard.peers]]
public_key = "<base64 peer public key>"  # REQUIRED. base64 32-byte key (the OTHER
                                   #   end's public key).
endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"     # EDGE: REQUIRED (this OR `endpoints`). ip:port
                                   #   literal (default, no DNS) OR hostname with
                                   #   opt-in `dns = true` below + an explicit [dns]
                                   #   resolver block. CONCENTRATOR: must be ABSENT
                                   #   (rejected) — it roams the edge dynamically.
                                   #   Mutually exclusive with `endpoints`.
# dns = false                       # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT false. Set true to enable
                                   #   hostname resolution for the endpoint above
                                   #   (requires an explicit [dns] resolver block).
# endpoints = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]
                                   # EDGE-ONLY ordered hub-failover list: [0] is the
                                   #   active concentrator, the rest ordered
                                   #   standbys. Each may be an ip:port literal
                                   #   (default) or a hostname with dns = true; when
                                   #   hostnames are used, each resolves to its full
                                   #   A+AAAA set and all addresses are ordered
                                   #   together in the failover list. duplicates
                                   #   rejected. Mutually exclusive with `endpoint`
                                   #   (its one-element form). Rejected on the
                                   #   concentrator.
allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]     # REQUIRED: >= 1 CIDR routed to this peer
                                   #   (enforced when the WG UAPI is built). This is
                                   #   the peer's inner tunnel address(es). A literal
                                   #   0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0 (full tunnel) is ALWAYS split
                                   #   into the equivalent /1+/1 pair at UAPI render —
                                   #   the engine never sees the literal /0.
# mode = "default-route"           # OPTIONAL, edge-only. Marks this peer as the
                                   #   edge's full-tunnel concentrator (opts into a
                                   #   0.0.0.0/0 / ::/0 allowed_ips entry above).
                                   #   Rejected on the concentrator. Config-surface
                                   #   only today: it does not itself install any
                                   #   OS-level default route.

# ── second peer example (hostname endpoint with DNS opt-in) ──────────────────────
# [[wireguard.peers]]
# public_key = "<base64 peer public key>"
# endpoint = "concentrator.example.com:51820"  # hostname endpoint (requires dns = true)
# dns = true                        # enable hostname resolution for this endpoint
#                                   #   (also requires an explicit [dns] resolver block)
# allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]

# ── amnezia obfuscation: OPTIONAL, OFF by default (plain WireGuard) ───────────
# ALL-OR-NOTHING: either omit the whole block, or set the entire jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2
# set — IDENTICALLY on both ends. One obfuscation engine per process.
# [amnezia]
# jc   = 4                         # junk packet count. > 0 when configured.
# jmin = 40                        # min junk packet size. > 0; jmin <= jmax.
# jmax = 70                        # max junk packet size. > 0.
# s1   = 30                        # init-packet junk prefix size. > 0.
# s2   = 40                        # response-packet junk prefix size. > 0.
                                   #   Constraint: (148 + s1) != (92 + s2) — the
                                   #   obfuscated init/response lengths must differ.
                                   #   (no defaults: required when the block present.)
# h1 = 1                           # magic header: initiation. Default 1..4 when the
# h2 = 2                           #   block is configured but NO header is given.
# h3 = 3                           #   When any is set, h1..h4 must be a DISTINCT set
# h4 = 4                           #   (values <= 4 mean "standard message type").

# ── scheduler: OPTIONAL. Omitted => active-backup, all knobs below ignored ────
# [scheduler]
# policy = "active-backup"         # "active-backup" (DEFAULT) | "weighted". Every
                                   #   knob below applies ONLY to "weighted"; under
                                   #   active-backup they are inert and left unset.
# per_path_capacity_fps = 10000.0  # DEFAULT 10000. Reference per-path capacity
                                   #   (frame slots/s): aggregation-gate denominator
                                   #   and pacing refill rate. > 0.
# engage_fraction    = 0.9         # DEFAULT 0.9. Engage aggregation above
                                   #   engage_fraction * capacity. In (0, 1].
# disengage_fraction = 0.5         # DEFAULT 0.5. Collapse below disengage_fraction
                                   #   * capacity. Must be in [0, engage_fraction) —
                                   #   the hysteresis band.
# collapse_dwell = "2s"            # DEFAULT 2s. Sustained-low dwell before
                                   #   collapsing to primary-only. >= 0.
# load_tau = "200ms"               # DEFAULT 200ms. Offered-load rate estimator time
                                   #   constant. > 0.
# pacing_enabled = false           # DEFAULT false. Turn on per-path send-pacing
                                   #   (token buckets). Off => buckets bypassed.
# pacing_burst_frames = 64.0       # DEFAULT 64. Token-bucket burst (frames). > 0
                                   #   when pacing_enabled.
# weight_rtt_floor = "1ms"         # DEFAULT 1ms. RTT floor in the weight formula. >0.
# weight_loss_floor = 0.001        # DEFAULT 1e-3. Loss floor under the sqrt in the
                                   #   weight formula. > 0.

# ── fec: OPTIONAL, OFF by default (no parity on the wire) ─────────────────────
# [fec]
# enabled = true                   # DEFAULT false. Turns the FEC plane on; when
                                   #   false every field below is ignored.
# data_shards = 10                 # K: inner datagrams per coding group. >= 1 when
                                   #   enabled. No default (required).
# parity_shards = 6                # M: parity frames per group (and the adaptive
                                   #   parity CEILING). >= 1 when enabled; K + M <=
                                   #   256. No default (required).
# deadline = "5ms"                 # DEFAULT 5ms. Partial-group flush deadline. > 0
                                   #   and <= 125ms.
# adaptive = false                 # DEFAULT false. Closed-loop parity controller
                                   #   (parity tracks measured loss up to the
                                   #   ceiling). Requires enabled = true.
# target_residual = 0.005          # ADAPTIVE-ONLY, primary sizing surface:
                                   #   post-recovery residual-loss SLA in (0, 1). No
                                   #   default. Mutually exclusive with safety_factor.
# safety_factor = 1.5              # ADAPTIVE-ONLY, legacy headroom multiplier >= 1.
                                   #   DEFAULTS to 1.5 when adaptive and NEITHER field
                                   #   is set. Must stay 0 in fixed mode. Mutually
                                   #   exclusive with target_residual.

# ── dns: OPTIONAL. Omitted => system resolver, default cadence/timeouts ───────
# Selects the TRANSPORT a peer's opt-in hostname endpoint (dns = true, above)
# is resolved through. [dns] alone never turns hostname resolution on.
#
# EXAMPLE 1 (IP-literal DoH resolver):
# [dns]
# resolver = "doh"                 # "system" | "doh" | "dot"
# doh_url = "https://198.51.100.1/dns-query"  # REQUIRED iff resolver = "doh"
# poll_interval = "30s"            # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT 30s. Re-resolution cadence.
# timeout = "5s"                   # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT 5s. Per-lookup timeout.
#
# EXAMPLE 2 (hostname DoT resolver, requires bootstrap_ip):
# [dns]
# resolver = "dot"                 # "system" | "doh" | "dot"
# dot_server = "dns.example.com:853"  # REQUIRED iff resolver = "dot"
# bootstrap_ip = "198.51.100.2"    # REQUIRED: dot_server's host is a hostname
                                   #   (BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant: must reach the
                                   #   resolver WITHOUT DNS lookup itself).
# poll_interval = "30s"
# timeout = "5s"
#
# EXAMPLE 3 (system resolver, any IP-literal endpoint):
# [dns]
# resolver = "system"              # DEFAULT "system". No URL/server/bootstrap needed.
# poll_interval = "30s"
# timeout = "5s"
#
# FULL [dns] BLOCK (EXAMPLE 1 — IP-literal DoH):
# [dns]
# resolver = "doh"                 # "system" (default) | "doh" | "dot".
# doh_url = "https://198.51.100.1/dns-query"  # REQUIRED iff resolver = "doh"
                                   #   Must be https://.
# dot_server = "198.51.100.1"      # REQUIRED iff resolver = "dot"; rejected
                                   #   under any other resolver. Bare host, or
                                   #   "host:port" where port must be 853 (the
                                   #   IANA-assigned DoT port).
# bootstrap_ip = "198.51.100.1"    # REQUIRED iff doh_url/dot_server's host is a
                                   #   hostname rather than an IP literal
                                   #   (BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant: the private
                                   #   resolver's own address must be reachable
                                   #   WITHOUT a DNS lookup). REJECTED (must stay
                                   #   absent) when the host is already an IP
                                   #   literal. No default.
# poll_interval = "30s"            # DEFAULT 30s. Re-resolution cadence for an
                                   #   opted-in hostname peer endpoint. > 0.
# timeout = "5s"                   # DEFAULT 5s. Per-lookup bound. > 0.

# ── metrics: OPTIONAL. Omit the block => no /metrics endpoint is served ───────
[metrics]
listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"          # No default. LOOPBACK-ONLY (127.0.0.0/8, ::1, or
                                   #   a hostname resolving only to loopback); any
                                   #   other address is REFUSED when the endpoint
                                   #   binds (not at load).

# ── log: OPTIONAL ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
[log]
level = "info"                     # DEFAULT "info" (empty => info). One of
                                   #   debug | info | warn | error ("warning"
                                   #   accepted for warn). Unknown => fail-fast.

# ── Cross-field constraints (not expressible inline) ─────────────────────────
# • Roles: `role` is required, never inferred. Edge-only: per-peer endpoint/
#   endpoints (required on edge, rejected on concentrator) + the pacing
#   declarations (dest_addr is edge-only in effect, merely inert on the
#   concentrator). Concentrator-only: wireguard.listen_port (required there).
# • endpoint vs endpoints: mutually exclusive; `endpoint` is the one-element form
#   of `endpoints`. On the edge exactly one must be present; the concentrator sets
#   neither. Entries are ip:port literals only (no DNS), de-duplicated.
# • Distinct source_addr: each [[paths]] needs a unique, valid source_addr
#   (compared unmapped, so 192.0.2.10 and ::ffff:192.0.2.10 collide). `name` must
#   also be unique.
# • allowed_ips: >= 1 CIDR per peer; empty is rejected when the WG config is
#   assembled (not by config.validate).
# • amnezia all-or-nothing: an unconfigured (all-zero) block is plain WireGuard.
#   Once ANY of jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2/h1..h4 is set, the full jc,jmin,jmax,s1,s2 set
#   must be > 0, jmin <= jmax, 148+s1 != 92+s2, and h1..h4 must be a distinct set
#   (omitted headers default to 1..4). Same profile on both ends. One engine/process.
# • link_bandwidth + link_rtt are a pair: both-or-neither, and all-or-nothing
#   across every path (declare on every [[paths]] block or none). They take effect
#   ONLY under scheduler.policy = "weighted" with pacing_enabled = true; otherwise
#   a declared bandwidth is inert. When active they are mutually exclusive with the
#   raw per_path_capacity_fps / pacing_burst_frames knobs.
# • scheduler off-unless-present: no [scheduler] => active-backup, every weighted
#   knob ignored. Under weighted, the hysteresis band requires
#   disengage_fraction < engage_fraction.
# • fec off-unless-present: no [fec] (or enabled = false) => no parity. adaptive =
#   true requires enabled = true. In adaptive mode set EITHER target_residual OR
#   safety_factor, never both; both must stay 0 (unset) in fixed mode.
# • metrics loopback-only: listen must be a loopback address (or a hostname
#   resolving entirely to loopback); a non-loopback bind is refused when the
#   endpoint starts. Omit the block to serve no metrics.
# • dns off-unless-present: no [dns] => system resolver, default cadence/
#   timeouts; [dns] only selects the transport a peer's `dns = true` opt-in
#   uses, never a gate by itself. doh_url/dot_server are mode-specific: set
#   exactly the one matching `resolver`, the other (and bootstrap_ip under
#   resolver = "system") must stay absent. BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant: a
#   hostname-form doh_url/dot_server host REQUIRES bootstrap_ip; an IP
#   literal host REJECTS a non-empty bootstrap_ip (mode mismatch).

-  // This demonstrates the DNS endpoints feature documented in docs/design.md.
+// readExampleFile returns the verbatim content of the repo's wanbond.example.toml,
+// so the tests below exercise the ACTUAL documented file rather than a hand-copied
+// literal that could silently drift from it.
+// extractExampleSection returns the text strictly between startMarker (exclusive —
+// the marker's own line, typically prose like "# EXAMPLE 1 (...):" or a section
+// header, is dropped) and endMarker (exclusive), with exactly one level of leading
+// "#" TOML-comment marker stripped from every line that has one at column 0. A
+// continuation line that is itself an aligned comment (e.g. wrapped explanatory
+// text with no leading "#" at column 0) is left untouched, mirroring exactly what a
+// human uncommenting the block by hand would do. This is how every commented-out
+// example in wanbond.example.toml is meant to be activated, so it is also the
+// correct way to extract and load one for testing.
+func extractExampleSection(t *testing.T, content, startMarker, endMarker string) string {
+    t.Fatalf("start marker %q not found in wanbond.example.toml", startMarker)
+      t.Fatalf("end marker %q not found after %q in wanbond.example.toml", endMarker, startMarker)
+  lines = lines[1:] // drop the marker's own (prose/header) line
+// placeholders with syntactically valid test key material, mirroring fill()'s
+// %PRIV%/%PUB%/%PSK% substitution for the hand-written fixtures above.
+// exampleTopSection returns wanbond.example.toml's content from the start up to
+    t.Fatal("live [[wireguard.peers]] header not found in wanbond.example.toml")
+// exampleLiteralPeer returns the live, IP-literal first peer block (public_key,
+// endpoint, allowed_ips, plus inert commented optional lines), with placeholders
+    t.Fatal("live [[wireguard.peers]] header not found in wanbond.example.toml")
+    t.Fatal("second peer example marker not found in wanbond.example.toml")
+// exampleHostnamePeer extracts and uncomments the documented "second peer example
+// (hostname endpoint with DNS opt-in)" block, with placeholders filled.
+    "# ── second peer example (hostname endpoint with DNS opt-in) ──",
+// exampleDNSBlock extracts and uncomments one of the three documented, mutually
+// exclusive [dns] EXAMPLE blocks by resolver mode.
+    "doh":    {"# EXAMPLE 1 (IP-literal DoH resolver):", "\n# EXAMPLE 2 (hostname DoT resolver, requires bootstrap_ip):"},
+    "dot":    {"# EXAMPLE 2 (hostname DoT resolver, requires bootstrap_ip):", "\n# EXAMPLE 3 (system resolver, any IP-literal endpoint):"},
+    "system": {"# EXAMPLE 3 (system resolver, any IP-literal endpoint):", "\n# Each EXAMPLE above is self-contained"},
+// TestExampleConfigLoads verifies that wanbond.example.toml's documented examples
+// — read from the ACTUAL file, not a hand-copied literal — parse correctly: the
+// live base config as shipped, the hostname-endpoint second peer example (with NO
+// [dns] block, confirming dns = true never requires one), and each of the three
+// mutually exclusive [dns] resolver-mode examples. A future edit that breaks any
+// of these documented examples fails this test.
+      t.Fatal("expected the live peer example to have dns=false (unset)")
+    // dns = true with NO [dns] block: must load and default to the system
+    // resolver — the [dns] block is OPTIONAL, never required by dns = true.
+      t.Fatalf("load hostname-peer example with no [dns] block: %v", err)
+      t.Fatal("expected a single hostname endpoint spec")
+      t.Fatalf("expected resolver to default to %q, got %q", DNSResolverSystem, cfg.DNS.Resolver)
+      body := top + exampleHostnamePeer(t, content) + "\n" + exampleDNSBlock(t, content, tc.mode)

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# wanbond

**wanbond** bonds two (or more) unreliable, heterogeneous WAN uplinks — e.g. a
low-latency-but-jittery Starlink link and a stable-but-metered 4G/5G link — into
a single resilient, DPI-resistant WireGuard tunnel for general IP traffic, with
adaptive forward error correction (FEC) that masks per-path loss without
duplicating every packet.

It is a single self-contained Go binary that runs on both ends of the tunnel:

- **edge** — a Linux box (behind a router) that bonds the local WAN uplinks;
- **concentrator** — a small public-IP VPS that terminates the tunnel and NATs
  traffic onward.

The same binary serves both roles; the role is chosen from the config file.

## What it gives you

In priority order (earlier properties never regress for later ones):

1. **Transparent failover** — a TCP flow survives a WAN dying mid-session, with
   no reset (WireGuard's roaming + our per-path liveness/failover).
2. **Data-thrift** — a metered link stays ~idle until it is actually needed.
3. **On-demand aggregation** — under load, traffic stripes across both links.
4. **FEC loss-masking** — Reed-Solomon parity reconstructs lost frames instead
   of retransmitting.
5. **Adaptive FEC** — redundancy tracks measured per-path loss (target a residual
   loss SLA, not a fixed overhead).
6. **DPI resistance** — the outer wire is unidentifiable high-entropy UDP: no
   WireGuard fingerprint, no magic bytes (nDPI/Suricata do not classify it as
   VPN/WireGuard).

## How it works, in one paragraph

wanbond embeds the **unmodified** [amneziawg-go](https://github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go)
WireGuard engine (TUN, Noise handshake, AEAD, rekey, roaming, keepalive) and puts
**all** bonding logic — multipath scheduling, an obfuscated outer frame codec,
Reed-Solomon FEC, a receive resequencer, and per-path telemetry — into a custom
`conn.Bind` that lives *beneath* the engine and operates only on opaque, already-
encrypted WireGuard datagrams. The engine sees one stable virtual endpoint; the
Bind privately fans traffic out across the real per-path UDP sockets. For the
full picture and the exact list of what we built on top of amneziawg-go, read
**[docs/design.md](docs/design.md)**.

## Quick start

Requires the dev shell (`nix develop`) which puts Go 1.26, golangci-lint, and the
netem/DPI test tooling on `PATH`.

```sh
just build          # go build ./...
just test           # unprivileged unit/property tests
just lint           # go vet + golangci-lint (incl. -tags e2e / -tags realhosts)
just release        # static linux amd64+arm64 binaries into dist/
```

Deploying the tunnel (build → install → config → systemd → firewall → metrics) is
covered per-topic in **[docs/install.md](docs/install.md)**; to provision a fresh
edge + concentrator (+ standby) from scratch, follow the operator-facing
**[pre-pilot rollout runbook](docs/runbook.md)**. The short version:

1. `just release`, then `install -m 0755 dist/wanbond-linux-<arch> /usr/local/bin/wanbond`.
2. Write `/etc/wanbond/config.toml` (mode **0600** — the daemon refuses looser
   permissions). Minimal shape:

   ```toml
   role = "edge"                    # or "concentrator"

   [[paths]]
   name        = "starlink"
   source_addr = "192.0.2.10"       # local IP this path's socket binds to
   [[paths]]
   name        = "cellular"
   source_addr = "192.0.2.20"

   [wireguard]
   private_key = "…"
   [[wireguard.peers]]
   public_key  = "…"
   endpoint    = "concentrator.example:51820"   # edge only; concentrator learns edges
   allowed_ips = ["10.10.0.0/24"]

   psk = "…"                        # outer control/probe PSK (not the WG PSK)

   # optional: [amnezia] (obfuscation, all-or-nothing), [fec], [scheduler], [dns], [metrics], [log]
   ```

3. Install the systemd unit for the role
   (`packaging/systemd/wanbond-{edge,concentrator}.service`), `daemon-reload`,
   `enable --now`.
4. On the **concentrator**, allow the tunnel interface through the firewall
   *ahead of* any default REJECT, and persist it across reboots (see install.md;
   `just realhosts-provision` automates the standing-testbed case).

## Operating it

- **Live reload**: `systemctl reload wanbond-…` (SIGHUP) re-reads the config and
  adds/removes paths without tearing the tunnel down.
- **Metrics**: set `[metrics].listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"` (loopback only — a
  non-loopback bind is refused) and scrape `/metrics` for per-path loss, FEC
  recovery, throughput, and probed RTT/liveness.
- **Logs**: structured, to stderr → `journalctl -u wanbond-…`.

## Testing

Three tiers (see [docs/design.md §Testing](docs/design.md) and
[docs/manual-checklist.md](docs/manual-checklist.md)):

| Tier | Command | What it covers |
|------|---------|----------------|
| unit / property | `just test` (`go test ./...`) | codec, FEC math, adaptive control law, anti-replay, schedulers, config |
| netns e2e | `just e2e` (`sudo -E go test -tags e2e ./test/e2e/...`) | two-netns tunnel bring-up, bonding, failover, FEC recovery, DPI audit (P0–P5) |
| real-host e2e | `just realhosts` (`-tags realhosts`) | two real machines over the internet (NAT edge + public concentrator); report-only |

> **Important fixture limitation:** the netns fixture is CPU/PPS-bound, so it
> validates *functional* bonding/FEC/failover/DPI but **cannot** measure
> real-link throughput aggregation or bufferbloat. Those must be measured on real
> uplinks (manual-checklist §P0) before a production rollout.

## Repository layout

```
cmd/wanbond/            entry point; role selection; SIGHUP reload
internal/bind/          the custom conn.Bind — multipath fan-out/coalesce, the amnezia boundary
internal/frame/         outer bonding frame codec (obfuscation + optional HMAC)
internal/sched/         send-side scheduler (active-backup, weighted, pacing)
internal/telemetry/     per-path PROBE/liveness, RTT/loss/jitter
internal/reseq/         receive resequencer (bounded-window reorder)
internal/fec/           Reed-Solomon FEC encoder/decoder
internal/adaptivefec/   closed-loop parity controller (target-residual SLA)
internal/config/        TOML load + fail-fast validation
internal/dnsresolve/    DNS resolution seam (Resolver interface, system + DoH + DoT impls, test fake)
internal/device/        tunnel lifecycle (Up/Down/Reload), metrics wiring
internal/metrics/       loopback Prometheus /metrics
internal/wireaudit/     requirement-6 DPI wire-format audit tooling
internal/log/           structured logging wrapper
test/e2e/               -tags e2e netns fixture (P0–P5)
test/realhosts/         -tags realhosts real-machine tier
docs/                   design, install, findings, manual checklist
```

## Status & limitations

The P0–P5 build is functionally complete, reviewed, and hardened. Known,
deliberate boundaries you must plan around:

- **Pacing is off by default (opt-in)** — enable it under `[scheduler]`
  (`pacing_enabled = true`) and size it from operator-declared per-link
  `link_bandwidth`/`link_rtt` (bandwidth-delay product); it was measured to
  eliminate bufferbloat on the bandwidth-capped netns fixture and the real-link
  tier. wanbond fixes the pace at config load and does not auto-tune it live (Q20).
- **Throughput aggregation and bufferbloat are not measured by the netns fixture**
  (it is CPU-bound) — the report-only real-link tier (`just p0-baseline`) measures
  them instead; validate on your own uplinks before a production rollout.
- **No live CONTROL protocol** — the frame type and its anti-replay guard exist,
  but inbound CONTROL is currently dropped (reserved for future signalling).
- **Multi-concentrator hub-failover: built and validated** — an
  edge peer may declare an ORDERED `endpoints` list (active concentrator + ordered
  standbys); the single `endpoint` form still works unchanged (its one-element
  case, which takes no failover action). On HUB LOSS (every path to the active
  concentrator down at once) the edge advances to the next endpoint, repoints the
  bond, and re-handshakes a fresh session (round-robin/wrap at end of list). The
  switch is covered by unit/component tests, the netns hub-failover e2e (T62), and
  the real-link mid-transfer WAN-kill tier (T63). Endpoints may be IP:port
  literals (default) or hostnames with opt-in `dns = true` per-peer and an
  explicit `[dns]` resolver block; see
  [docs/design.md §DNS endpoints and resolver privacy trade-offs](docs/design.md).
- **UDP only** — obfuscation defeats DPI *classification*, not a wholesale UDP
  block; there is no TCP/TLS fallback.
- **DATA/PARITY frames are unauthenticated by design** (inner WireGuard
  authenticates the real payload; a DoS-grade forgery risk is accepted). PROBE
  and CONTROL are PSK-HMAC authenticated.

See [docs/design.md §Security model](docs/design.md) and
[docs/p0-findings.md](docs/p0-findings.md) for the reasoning behind each.

## Documentation

- **[docs/design.md](docs/design.md)** — architecture and exactly what we built on
  top of amneziawg-go.
- **[docs/install.md](docs/install.md)** — full setup and operation (per-topic
  reference); §3z is the exhaustive all-keys config reference.
- **[wanbond.example.toml](wanbond.example.toml)** — copy-pasteable annotated
  example config with every key, its default, and its constraints.
- **[docs/runbook.md](docs/runbook.md)** — pre-pilot rollout runbook: provision a
  fresh edge + concentrator (+ standby) from scratch, end to end.
- **[docs/manual-checklist.md](docs/manual-checklist.md)** — manual per-phase and
  real-link verification checklist.
- **[docs/p0-findings.md](docs/p0-findings.md)** / **[docs/p0-checkpoint.md](docs/p0-checkpoint.md)**
  — the P0 spike findings that fixed several load-bearing design decisions
  (single virtual endpoint, resequencing, why the fixture is CPU-bound).
- **[AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md)** — instructions for AI agents working in this repo
  (including the rule to keep these docs in sync with the code).

lines: bond, and re-handshakes a fresh session (round-robin/wrap at end of list). The
switch is covered by unit/component tests, the netns hub-failover e2e (T62), and
the real-link mid-transfer WAN-kill tier (T63). Endpoints may be IP:port
-  literals (default) or hostnames with opt-in `dns = true` per-peer and an
+  literals (default) or hostnames with per-peer opt-in `dns = true`; the
+  `[dns]` resolver block is OPTIONAL — an absent block defaults to the system
+  resolver — and only selects the transport (system/DoH/DoT) that opt-in
- **UDP only** — obfuscation defeats DPI *classification*, not a wholesale UDP
block; there is no TCP/TLS fallback.

425	## DNS endpoints and resolver privacy trade-offs
426	
427	The optional `[dns]` block selects which transport resolves a peer's hostname
428	endpoint when opted in with `dns = true` per-peer. It is **opt-in by default —
429	[dns] alone never enables hostname resolution**; every peer endpoint is always
430	an IP literal unless explicitly marked `dns = true`. Hostname endpoints are
431	resolved through the OS system resolver by default (when `[dns]` is absent).
432	
433	### Why default-off: the DPI thesis
434	
435	A **pre-tunnel hostname lookup** is an unencrypted (cleartext) signal: an
436	on-path adversary sees the edge asking for the public concentrator's hostname
437	*before* the tunnel is up, making the host blocklistable at the DNS level
438	without inspecting any encrypted traffic. This is true regardless of how the
439	lookup is done — system resolver, DoH/DoT — because the resolver *itself*
440	learns the query. The default posture (IP literals only; hostnames deferred
441	to an explicit opt-in with `dns = true` + an explicit `[dns]` resolver block)
442	keeps this leakage off by default and surfaces it as an intentional choice.
443	
444	### Leaked artifacts per resolver mode
445	
446	Once a peer opts into hostname resolution (`dns = true`), the transport choice
447	in `[dns].resolver` determines what information escapes to a passive on-path
448	observer — per Requirement-6 testing (test/e2e/p5_dpi_test.go, Q29/Q33):
449	
450	- **system** (default, OS stub resolver): A **cleartext DNS query naming the
451	  concentrator's hostname** — the full QNAME in plaintext on port 53. This is
452	  the most visible artifact: a DPI engine observes the exact hostname being
453	  resolved and can block it pre-emptively at the network edge.
454	
455	- **DoH** (DNS-over-HTTPS, RFC 8484): The **TLS ClientHello SNI** (Server Name
456	  Indication, naming the DoH provider's host) **plus timing/connection metadata**
457	  to the DoH provider. The query payload itself is encrypted within the HTTPS
458	  tunnel, but the SNI and the observed request/response cadence allow timing-based
459	  inference and correlation to the DoH provider — the concentrator hostname is
460	  *not* visible on the wire.
461	
462	- **DoT** (DNS-over-TLS, RFC 7858): Identical to DoH: the **TLS ClientHello SNI**
463	  (naming the DoT server) **plus timing and connection metadata** to the DoT
464	  provider. The query is encrypted, but SNI + timing correlates the edge to that
465	  resolver.
466	
467	In all three cases, **multi-record expansions** (a hostname resolving to multiple
468	A/AAAA records, e.g. concentrator failover `endpoints` list) feed back into
469	hub-failover: each address in the result set feeds the active-backup or weighted
470	scheduler as a separate routable endpoint, so the edge can failover within that
471	set on liveness loss (one address down → try the next).
472	
473	### Opt-in defer-and-reconcile boot semantics
474	
475	A hostname endpoint that cannot be resolved at startup (resolver down, DNS
476	outage, network unreachable) **never blocks tunnel bring-up**. The tunnel
477	boots without that endpoint, and a background re-resolution loop (at the
478	cadence `[dns].poll_interval`, default 30s) installs it and initiates the
479	Noise handshake on the first successful lookup. Steady-state re-resolution then
480	repoints the bond whenever the hostname's record changes (TTL-driven or faster,
481	per the operational `poll_interval`).
482	
483	- **Re-resolution cadence**: `[dns].poll_interval` (default 30s, must be > 0).
484	  Governs how often an opted-in hostname endpoint is re-resolved; changes are
485	  reconciled immediately.
486	
487	- **Liveness-loss trigger**: If all endpoints (resolved A/AAAA records + any
488	  IP-literal endpoints) for a peer go unreachable simultaneously, the
489	  re-resolution loop shortens its cadence and retries aggressively; once any
490	  endpoint recovers, it returns to the normal cadence.
491	
492	- **Change suppression**: Re-resolution results that are identical to the prior
493	  set (same IP + port, same order) do not trigger re-keying or re-handshake —
494	  they are silent, minimizing control churn.
495	
496	### Mixing rules with ordered endpoints
497	
498	When a peer declares multiple endpoints via the `endpoints` list (hub-failover),
499	and one or more are hostnames (`dns = true`), the following rules apply:
500	
501	1. **Each hostname expands to its full A+AAAA record set** at resolve time; IP
502	   literals and resolved addresses are all ordered together per the scheduler's
503	   active-backup or weighted policy.
504	
505	2. **Order preservation**: the `endpoints` list order is **strict** — the scheduler
506	   respects it for active-backup failover (index 0 is primary, index N are standbys).
507	   When a hostname at index K resolves to multiple addresses, they are inserted
508	   *consecutively* in that order (first address fills index K, subsequent addresses
509	   shift later entries right in the ordering).
510	
511	3. **Deduplication**: if two `endpoints` entries (whether IP or hostname) resolve
512	   to the same address:port, both are rejected at config load (duplicates not
513	   allowed in the flattened set).
514	
515	4. **Resolver selection is global**: `[dns].resolver` applies to *all* opted-in

## DNS endpoints and resolver privacy trade-offs

The optional `[dns]` block selects which transport resolves a peer's hostname
endpoint when opted in with `dns = true` per-peer. It is **opt-in by default —
[dns] alone never enables hostname resolution**; every peer endpoint is always
an IP literal unless explicitly marked `dns = true`. Hostname endpoints are
resolved through the OS system resolver by default (when `[dns]` is absent).

### Why default-off: the DPI thesis

A **pre-tunnel hostname lookup** is an unencrypted (cleartext) signal: an
on-path adversary sees the edge asking for the public concentrator's hostname
*before* the tunnel is up, making the host blocklistable at the DNS level
without inspecting any encrypted traffic. This is true regardless of how the
lookup is done — system resolver, DoH/DoT — because the resolver *itself*
learns the query. The default posture (IP literals only; hostnames deferred
to an explicit opt-in with `dns = true` + an explicit `[dns]` resolver block)
keeps this leakage off by default and surfaces it as an intentional choice.

### Leaked artifacts per resolver mode

Once a peer opts into hostname resolution (`dns = true`), the transport choice
in `[dns].resolver` determines what information escapes to a passive on-path
observer — per Requirement-6 testing (test/e2e/p5_dpi_test.go, Q29/Q33):

- **system** (default, OS stub resolver): A **cleartext DNS query naming the
  concentrator's hostname** — the full QNAME in plaintext on port 53. This is
  the most visible artifact: a DPI engine observes the exact hostname being
  resolved and can block it pre-emptively at the network edge.

- **DoH** (DNS-over-HTTPS, RFC 8484): The **TLS ClientHello SNI** (Server Name
  Indication, naming the DoH provider's host) **plus timing/connection metadata**
  to the DoH provider. The query payload itself is encrypted within the HTTPS
  tunnel, but the SNI and the observed request/response cadence allow timing-based
  inference and correlation to the DoH provider — the concentrator hostname is
  *not* visible on the wire.

- **DoT** (DNS-over-TLS, RFC 7858): Identical to DoH: the **TLS ClientHello SNI**
  (naming the DoT server) **plus timing and connection metadata** to the DoT
  provider. The query is encrypted, but SNI + timing correlates the edge to that
  resolver.

In all three cases, **multi-record expansions** (a hostname resolving to multiple
A/AAAA records, e.g. concentrator failover `endpoints` list) feed back into
hub-failover: each address in the result set feeds the active-backup or weighted
scheduler as a separate routable endpoint, so the edge can failover within that
set on liveness loss (one address down → try the next).

### Opt-in defer-and-reconcile boot semantics

A hostname endpoint that cannot be resolved at startup (resolver down, DNS
outage, network unreachable) **never blocks tunnel bring-up**. The tunnel
boots without that endpoint, and a background re-resolution loop (at the
cadence `[dns].poll_interval`, default 30s) installs it and initiates the
Noise handshake on the first successful lookup. Steady-state re-resolution then
repoints the bond whenever the hostname's record changes (TTL-driven or faster,
per the operational `poll_interval`).

- **Re-resolution cadence**: `[dns].poll_interval` (default 30s, must be > 0).
  Governs how often an opted-in hostname endpoint is re-resolved; changes are
  reconciled immediately.

- **Liveness-loss trigger**: If all endpoints (resolved A/AAAA records + any
  IP-literal endpoints) for a peer go unreachable simultaneously, the
  re-resolution loop shortens its cadence and retries aggressively; once any
  endpoint recovers, it returns to the normal cadence.

- **Change suppression**: Re-resolution results that are identical to the prior
  set (same IP + port, same order) do not trigger re-keying or re-handshake —
  they are silent, minimizing control churn.

### Mixing rules with ordered endpoints

When a peer declares multiple endpoints via the `endpoints` list (hub-failover),
and one or more are hostnames (`dns = true`), the following rules apply:

1. **Each hostname expands to its full A+AAAA record set** at resolve time; IP
   literals and resolved addresses are all ordered together per the scheduler's
   active-backup or weighted policy.

2. **Order preservation**: the `endpoints` list order is **strict** — the scheduler
   respects it for active-backup failover (index 0 is primary, index N are standbys).
   When a hostname at index K resolves to multiple addresses, they are inserted
   *consecutively* in that order (first address fills index K, subsequent addresses
   shift later entries right in the ordering).

3. **Deduplication**: if two `endpoints` entries (whether IP or hostname) resolve
   to the same address:port, both are rejected at config load (duplicates not
   allowed in the flattened set).

4. **Resolver selection is global**: `[dns].resolver` applies to *all* opted-in
   hostname endpoints — you cannot mix system + DoH + DoT within one config.
   Choose one transport per tunnel.

## Load-bearing invariants

These are the rules that keep the design correct; break them and the tunnel
misbehaves subtly. Agents and contributors must preserve them.

1. **One virtual endpoint per peer (A1).** The engine sees a single stable

content.content:
129	// failoverSpec is one ordered endpoint entry together with its CURRENT expansion. A
130	// literal entry's expansion is the fixed single AddrPort; a hostname entry's expansion is
131	// its latest resolved record set (Q32 multi-record), swapped in place by updateResolution.
132	// The spec itself (config.EndpointSpec) is retained so the controller can tell a hostname
133	// spec — which may grow/shrink at runtime — from an immutable literal, e.g. when deciding
134	// whether the failover loop may ever have work to do.
135	type failoverSpec struct {
136		spec  config.EndpointSpec
137		addrs []netip.AddrPort
138	}
139	
140	// newHubFailover builds the controller over an ordered list of IP-LITERAL endpoints — the
141	// legacy all-literal form (each address becomes its own single-entry literal spec). It is
142	// a pure constructor (no goroutine, no engine dependency beyond the injected collaborators)
143	// so its decision logic is unit-testable against a fake clock, fake health, and fake
144	// remote/rehandshake.
145	func newHubFailover(endpoints []netip.AddrPort, health []hubHealth, remote peerRemote, rh rehandshake, clock telemetry.Clock, settle time.Duration, lg log.Logger) *hubFailover {
146		specs := make([]failoverSpec, len(endpoints))
147		for i, ap := range endpoints {
148			specs[i] = failoverSpec{spec: config.EndpointSpec{Addr: ap}, addrs: []netip.AddrPort{ap}}
149		}
150		// An all-literal controller starts with every spec's expansion non-empty, so activeSpec is set
151		// at construction and the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH (h.install in updateResolution) is never
152		// reached — it takes no engine-endpoint install. Pass a no-op for the required install collaborator.
153		return newHubFailoverFromSpecs(specs, health, remote, rh, func(netip.AddrPort) {}, clock, settle, lg)
154	}
155	
156	// newHubFailoverFromSpecs builds the controller over the ordered, spec-keyed endpoint set.
157	// The active endpoint is initialized to the FIRST flattened entry (spec order); when every
158	// spec's expansion is empty (e.g. a hostname-only peer before the resolver has run) there
159	// is no active entry yet (activeSpec == -1) and check takes no action until an expansion
160	// appears. Like newHubFailover it injects no goroutine or engine dependency. install is a
161	// REQUIRED collaborator (the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH, R70): making it a constructor
162	// parameter — rather than a field patched in afterwards — turns a lost production wiring line
163	// into a compile error instead of a silent degradation to SetPeerRemote.
164	func newHubFailoverFromSpecs(specs []failoverSpec, health []hubHealth, remote peerRemote, rh rehandshake, install func(netip.AddrPort), clock telemetry.Clock, settle time.Duration, lg log.Logger) *hubFailover {
165		h := &hubFailover{
166			specs:       specs,
167			health:      health,
168			remote:      remote,
169			rehandshake: rh,
170			install:     install,
171			clock:       clock,
172			settle:      settle,
173			lastSwitch:  clock.Now(),
174			log:         lg,
175		}
176		h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr = h.entryAtLocked(0)
177		h.idx = h.flatIndexLocked(h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr)
178		return h
179	}
180	
181	// flatLenLocked is the number of endpoints in the FLATTENED list (the sum of every spec's
182	// current expansion length). Caller holds mu.
183	func (h *hubFailover) flatLenLocked() int {
184		n := 0
185		for i := range h.specs {
186			n += len(h.specs[i].addrs)
187		}
188		return n
189	}
190	
191	// canFailoverLocked reports whether the controller could EVER take a failover action: the
192	// flattened list already holds >= 2 endpoints, or SOME spec is a hostname whose runtime
193	// resolution may yet grow it. An all-literal set with a single entry can never grow, so its
194	// loop is a pure no-op and need not run. Caller holds mu.
195	func (h *hubFailover) canFailoverLocked() bool {
196		if h.flatLenLocked() >= 2 {
197			return true
198		}

content.text: Now fix the "Mixing rules" section:

input.old_string:
1. **Each hostname expands to its full A+AAAA record set** at resolve time; IP
   literals and resolved addresses are all ordered together per the scheduler's
   active-backup or weighted policy.

2. **Order preservation**: the `endpoints` list order is **strict** — the scheduler
   respects it for active-backup failover (index 0 is primary, index N are standbys).
   When a hostname at index K resolves to multiple addresses, they are inserted
   *consecutively* in that order (first address fills index K, subsequent addresses
   shift later entries right in the ordering).

3. **Deduplication**: if two `endpoints` entries (whether IP or hostname) resolve
   to the same address:port, both are rejected at config load (duplicates not
   allowed in the flattened set).

4. **Resolver selection is global**: `[dns].resolver` applies to *all* opted-in
   hostname endpoints — you cannot mix system + DoH + DoT within one config.
   Choose one transport per tunnel.
input.new_string:
1. **Each hostname expands to its full A+AAAA record set** at resolve time, but
   the resolver's own answer order is **not** preserved: `orderAddrPorts`
   deterministically re-orders that spec's addresses IPv4-first-then-IPv6, and
   drops any address of a family no local path can source (a v4-only edge
   drops AAAA answers) — so an unchanged DNS answer always yields a
   byte-identical expansion, independent of the resolver's own record order.
   Endpoint *selection* is always ordered **ACTIVE-STANDBY** — `hubFailover`
   advances through the flattened, ordered list on hub loss; `[scheduler]`'s
   weighted policy is never consulted here (see above).

2. **Order preservation**: the `endpoints` list order is **strict** — index 0
   is the active concentrator, index N are ordered standbys, and hub-failover
   advances through them in that order on hub loss. When a hostname at index K
   resolves to multiple addresses, they are inserted *consecutively* in that
   order (first address fills index K, subsequent addresses shift later
   entries right in the flattened ordering) — but, per rule 1, in the
   family-ordered form, not raw resolver order.

3. **Deduplication is per-namespace and load-time only; resolved addresses are
   never deduplicated across specs.** At config load, `resolveEndpoints`
   rejects a duplicate **within** each of two disjoint namespaces — a literal
   duplicating another literal, or a hostname:port duplicating another
   hostname:port — never across the two, and never on a resolved address (no
   resolution happens at load, Q30). At runtime, `orderAddrPorts` dedupes
   addresses only **within a single hostname spec's own** A+AAAA answer. Two
   *different* specs (two distinct hostnames, or a hostname and a literal)
   that happen to resolve or point to the same address:port are **not**
   rejected or merged — both remain distinct entries in the flattened
   failover list.

4. **Resolver selection is global**: `[dns].resolver` applies to *all* opted-in
   hostname endpoints — you cannot mix system + DoH + DoT within one config.
   Choose one transport per tunnel.

file.content:
Two configuration facts make it work:

1. **The edge declares an ORDERED endpoint list.** Replace the single
   `endpoint` on the edge's peer block with `endpoints` — index 0 is the
   active/primary hub, the rest are ordered standbys tried in order:

   ```toml
   [[wireguard.peers]]
   public_key  = "<base64 CONCENTRATOR public key>"
   endpoints   = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]  # [active, standby, ...]
   allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]
   ```

   `endpoints` is **mutually exclusive** with `endpoint`; the single `endpoint`
   form is just its one-element case and takes **no** failover action, so a
   one-concentrator deployment stays exactly as in step 2. Endpoints are
   **IP:port only — no DNS resolution**.

2. **The standby shares the active's WireGuard static key.** All hubs in the

file.content:
role = "edge"
psk = "<base64 32-byte outer-control PSK, same on both ends>"

# One [[paths]] block per WAN uplink. source_addr pins the path's UDP socket
# to the local source IP the upstream router routes out the intended WAN.
[[paths]]
name = "starlink"
source_addr = "192.168.1.10"

[[paths]]
name = "5g"
source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
# dest_addr = "203.0.113.7:51820"  # optional per-path concentrator endpoint;
                                   # omit when one public IP fronts all uplinks

[wireguard]
private_key = "<base64 edge private key>"

[[wireguard.peers]]
public_key = "<base64 concentrator public key>"
endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"     # required on the edge
# endpoints = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]  # ordered hub-failover
# form (Q18): first entry is the active/primary concentrator, the rest are
# ordered standbys tried in order when the active one is lost. Mutually
# exclusive with `endpoint` above — `endpoint` is just its one-element form,
# so a single-concentrator deployment keeps using it unchanged. Edge-only.
# Hub failover (all-paths-down detection + switch/re-handshake to the next
# endpoint) is implemented: T54 config surface + T57 switch.
allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]     # concentrator's inner tunnel address

[metrics]
listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"          # loopback only; the daemon rejects anything else

[log]
level = "info"
```

### Concentrator config (`/etc/wanbond/concentrator.toml`)

```toml

content.text: Now let's check the §Optional [dns] resolver block section (~L206) that cross-references "the endpoint list note above":

content.content:
205	- It is **all-or-nothing**: declare `link_bandwidth` (and `link_rtt`) on *every*
206	  path or none. The shared per-path pace is sized to the **slowest declared link**
207	  (the bottleneck), so a partial declaration is rejected.
208	- A declared bandwidth with `pacing_enabled = false` (the default) is **inert** —
209	  the synthetic default pace is kept and the tunnel behaves as before.
210	- `link_bandwidth` is **mutually exclusive** with the raw `per_path_capacity_fps` /
211	  `pacing_burst_frames` knobs: declare the link bandwidth *or* set the frame-slot
212	  knobs, not both. A non-positive or unparseable bandwidth/RTT is rejected at load.
213	
214	### Optional `[dns]` resolver block
215	
216	Hostname peer endpoints (opt-in per peer with `dns = true`, see the endpoint
217	list note above) are resolved through the OS **system resolver by default** —
218	an absent `[dns]` block is inert. To route that resolution through a private
219	DNS-over-HTTPS or DNS-over-TLS resolver instead, add an explicit `[dns]`
220	block:
221	
222	```toml
223	[dns]
224	resolver = "doh"                                # "system" (default) | "doh" | "dot"
225	doh_url = "https://198.51.100.1/dns-query"       # required iff resolver = "doh"
226	# dot_server = "198.51.100.1"                    # required iff resolver = "dot"
227	# poll_interval = "30s"                          # re-resolution cadence; must be > 0
228	# timeout = "5s"                                 # per-lookup bound; must be > 0
229	```
230	
231	- `[dns]` only **selects the transport** the per-peer `dns = true` opt-in uses;
232	  it never turns hostname resolution on by itself.
233	- **BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant**: `doh_url`/`dot_server`'s host must itself be
234	  reachable *without* a DNS lookup (otherwise resolving your private
235	  resolver's own name would need the very system resolver you configured it
236	  to avoid). Give it as an IP literal, or set `bootstrap_ip` explicitly when
237	  it is a hostname — config load fails fast otherwise. `bootstrap_ip` must
238	  stay absent when the host is already an IP literal; a non-empty value
239	  there is rejected at load as a mode mismatch.
240	- `dot_server` dials the fixed IANA-assigned DoT port (853); an explicit
241	  `host:port` form must use that exact port.
242	- `doh_url`/`dot_server` are mode-specific: setting the wrong one for the
243	  selected `resolver` (or either one under `resolver = "system"`) is
244	  rejected at load.
245	- **Tolerant boot**: a hostname endpoint that cannot be resolved at startup
246	  (resolver down, DNS outage) never fails bring-up — the tunnel comes up
247	  *without* that peer endpoint and the background re-resolution loop installs
248	  it and initiates the handshake on the first successful lookup. Steady-state
249	  re-resolution then repoints the bond whenever the record changes.

file.content:
- It is **all-or-nothing**: declare `link_bandwidth` (and `link_rtt`) on *every*
  path or none. The shared per-path pace is sized to the **slowest declared link**
  (the bottleneck), so a partial declaration is rejected.
- A declared bandwidth with `pacing_enabled = false` (the default) is **inert** —
  the synthetic default pace is kept and the tunnel behaves as before.
- `link_bandwidth` is **mutually exclusive** with the raw `per_path_capacity_fps` /
  `pacing_burst_frames` knobs: declare the link bandwidth *or* set the frame-slot
  knobs, not both. A non-positive or unparseable bandwidth/RTT is rejected at load.

### Optional `[dns]` resolver block

Hostname peer endpoints (opt-in per peer with `dns = true`, see the endpoint
list note above) are resolved through the OS **system resolver by default** —
an absent `[dns]` block is inert. To route that resolution through a private
DNS-over-HTTPS or DNS-over-TLS resolver instead, add an explicit `[dns]`
block:

```toml
[dns]
resolver = "doh"                                # "system" (default) | "doh" | "dot"
doh_url = "https://198.51.100.1/dns-query"       # required iff resolver = "doh"
# dot_server = "198.51.100.1"                    # required iff resolver = "dot"
# poll_interval = "30s"                          # re-resolution cadence; must be > 0
# timeout = "5s"                                 # per-lookup bound; must be > 0
```

- `[dns]` only **selects the transport** the per-peer `dns = true` opt-in uses;
  it never turns hostname resolution on by itself.
- **BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant**: `doh_url`/`dot_server`'s host must itself be
  reachable *without* a DNS lookup (otherwise resolving your private
  resolver's own name would need the very system resolver you configured it
  to avoid). Give it as an IP literal, or set `bootstrap_ip` explicitly when
  it is a hostname — config load fails fast otherwise. `bootstrap_ip` must
  stay absent when the host is already an IP literal; a non-empty value
  there is rejected at load as a mode mismatch.
- `dot_server` dials the fixed IANA-assigned DoT port (853); an explicit
  `host:port` form must use that exact port.
- `doh_url`/`dot_server` are mode-specific: setting the wrong one for the
  selected `resolver` (or either one under `resolver = "system"`) is
  rejected at load.
- **Tolerant boot**: a hostname endpoint that cannot be resolved at startup
  (resolver down, DNS outage) never fails bring-up — the tunnel comes up
  *without* that peer endpoint and the background re-resolution loop installs
  it and initiates the handshake on the first successful lookup. Steady-state
  re-resolution then repoints the bond whenever the record changes.

input.new_string:
- **`endpoint` vs `endpoints`.** Mutually exclusive; `endpoint` is the
  one-element form of `endpoints`. On the edge exactly one must be present; the
  concentrator must set neither. Each entry is an `ip:port` literal (default)
  or, with the peer's `dns = true` opt-in, a hostname resolved at runtime
  (never at config load); duplicates are rejected within each form

content.content:
### Why default-off: the DPI thesis

A **pre-tunnel hostname lookup** is an unencrypted (cleartext) signal: an
on-path adversary sees the edge asking for the public concentrator's hostname
*before* the tunnel is up, making the host blocklistable at the DNS level
without inspecting any encrypted traffic. This is true regardless of how the
lookup is done — system resolver, DoH/DoT — because the resolver *itself*
learns the query. The default posture (IP literals only; hostnames deferred
to an explicit opt-in with `dns = true`) keeps this leakage off by default and
surfaces it as an intentional choice. The `[dns]` block is **optional** even
once a peer opts in — it only selects the resolver *transport*; an absent
